sections.name: Pharmaceutical Quality System & Governance
sections.intro: An investigator or auditor in this area is testing whether a real, documented quality system governs the operation rather than a binder that exists only for inspections. Expect them to trace the quality policy and objectives down to evidence that management actually monitors performance, allocates resources, and holds defined accountability, including over work done by contract partners.

questions.q: Is the pharmaceutical quality system described in a current, approved document (for example a quality manual or equivalent) that defines its scope, the products and sites it covers, and how its elements connect?

Does the quality system explicitly cover the full product lifecycle stages relevant to your operation (development, technology transfer, commercial manufacturing, product discontinuation) rather than only routine commercial production?
Is there a documented, current quality policy that has been approved by senior management and that states the organization's intentions and direction for quality?
Has the quality policy been communicated to and understood by personnel at all levels, with evidence such as training records, postings, or onboarding documentation?
Have measurable quality objectives been established that align with the quality policy, and are they translated into specific targets at the relevant functions and levels?
Are quality objectives reviewed on a defined frequency, with documented evidence of progress against them and action taken when targets are missed?
Is senior (executive) management's leadership and commitment to the quality system documented, including their participation in management review rather than delegation to quality alone?

Is there a defined organizational structure with current organization charts that show reporting lines, and is the quality unit's independence from manufacturing and commercial pressure clearly established?

Are roles, responsibilities, and authorities for quality-impacting activities documented (for example in job descriptions, a responsibilities matrix, or SOPs) and communicated to the individuals who hold them?
Is there a designated, documented individual with overall responsibility and authority for the quality system, with the authority to stop or release product as appropriate?
Does management review occur on a defined periodic schedule with a documented procedure specifying the required inputs, attendees, and outputs?

Do management review inputs include the recognized performance and quality indicators (such as deviation and CAPA status, complaints and recalls, change management, audit and inspection results, supplier performance, and stability or product-quality trends)?
Do management reviews produce documented decisions, assigned actions with owners and due dates, and is closure of those actions tracked to completion?
Is there a defined set of quality metrics or key performance indicators that management uses to monitor process performance and product quality, with established alert and action thresholds?
Are quality metrics trended over time (not just reported as point-in-time snapshots) so that adverse trends are detected before they become failures?
Is there a documented continual improvement mechanism in which management review outputs, trends, and risk assessments feed concrete improvements to processes and the quality system itself?

Is a documented risk management process (consistent with ICH Q9 principles) integrated into quality system decisions such as change control, deviation handling, and supplier oversight, rather than treated as a separate one-time exercise?

Has management formally assessed and documented that resources (personnel, facilities, equipment, and budget) are adequate to operate and maintain the quality system, and is this revisited when scope or volume changes?
Are personnel performing quality-impacting activities qualified by a combination of education, training, and experience, with current records demonstrating competence for their assigned tasks?
Is training completed and assessed for effectiveness before personnel perform unsupervised GxP work, per a role-based curriculum?
Is there a defined process to identify and provide additional resources or expertise when workload, backlogs (for example open deviations or overdue CAPAs), or new activities exceed current capacity?

Does a controlled document management system govern creation, review, approval, distribution, revision, and retirement of quality system documents, so only the current approved version is available at the point of use?
Are SOPs and other controlled documents reviewed and reapproved on a defined periodic cycle, with obsolete versions promptly removed from use?
Is there a documented procedure governing how quality system records are retained, retrievable for the required retention period, and protected from loss or unauthorized alteration?

Are outsourced GxP activities (contract manufacturing, testing, packaging, distribution, and similar) covered by current written quality agreements that clearly define quality responsibilities between you and each external party?
Do you formally qualify contract partners and suppliers of GxP materials and services before use, based on documented risk assessment and evidence of their capability?

Do you maintain an approved supplier and contractor list and monitor ongoing performance of outsourced parties (for example through periodic review, metrics, or audits) rather than relying on the initial qualification alone?

Is there documented evidence that you retain oversight and final decision authority over product quality and release even when activities are outsourced, with a defined process to evaluate proposed changes at contract sites?
Does your audit program include periodic audits of critical suppliers and contract organizations on a risk-based schedule, with findings tracked to closure?
Do you operate an internal (self) audit program against a defined schedule that covers the quality system elements and GxP areas, conducted by independent and objective personnel?
Are internal and external audit findings entered into the CAPA or action-tracking system, prioritized by risk, and closed with verified effectiveness rather than just closed administratively?

Is there a defined process for managing knowledge across the product lifecycle (for example development history, process understanding, and lessons learned) so that this information informs decisions and transfers between sites and functions?

Is there a documented change management system that captures changes to facilities, equipment, processes, methods, suppliers, and the quality system itself, with risk-based evaluation and approval before implementation?

Does the quality system define how state of control is established and maintained for processes, including escalation paths so that loss of control or significant quality issues reach senior management promptly?

Is there a defined and documented process for managing the quality aspects of product discontinuation (covering handling of records, retention samples, ongoing stability, and continued supply obligations)?

For investigational products, does the quality system scale appropriately to the development phase while still controlling the critical elements (release, traceability, and quality oversight) for material used in clinical studies?

Where activities are governed by a parent company or a global quality system, is it documented how corporate quality standards apply to the local site and how local management retains accountability for site quality?

sections.name: Quality Unit & Independence
sections.intro: An investigator will probe whether a defined quality unit actually holds the authority to approve and reject materials, components, batches, procedures, and specifications, and whether it operates independently of production pressure. Expect verification that the unit's responsibilities are written down, that those written procedures are followed in practice, and that release and reject decisions trace back to qualified, authorized people with documented rationale.

questions.q: Is there a quality unit formally established with defined responsibilities and authority documented in a current, approved organizational document or charter?

Do written procedures clearly assign to the quality unit the responsibility to approve or reject all components, drug product containers, closures, in-process materials, packaging materials, labeling, and finished products?
Does the quality unit have the documented authority to approve or reject products or materials manufactured, processed, packaged, or held under contract by another company?
Is the quality unit's authority to reject any batch or material absolute, meaning no production or commercial role can overrule a reject decision?
Is batch release for distribution performed only by an authorized, named person in the quality unit after a documented review confirming all release criteria are met?
Before release, does the quality unit review the batch record to confirm the batch was produced and controlled in accordance with the approved master production and control records?
Does the quality unit verify that all required in-process and finished-product specifications and acceptance criteria were met before a batch is released?
Are deviations, out-of-specification results, and unexplained discrepancies affecting a batch investigated and dispositioned by the quality unit before that batch is released?
Does the quality unit report to senior management through a line that is independent of, and not subordinate to, manufacturing or production management?
Are the people who release product organizationally separate from those who have production output, schedule, or cost responsibility for that product?
Are quality unit decisions on disposition free from documented or observable pressure tied to production targets, shipping deadlines, or financial incentives?
Are all written procedures and changes to procedures affecting the identity, strength, quality, and purity of the drug product reviewed and approved by the quality unit?
Does the quality unit review and approve all specifications, standards, sampling plans, and test procedures, and any changes to them?
Are the quality unit's own written procedures (for review and approval, release, investigation, and other functions) the current approved versions, and the only versions in use at the point of use?
Is there documented evidence (such as release records that follow the required procedural steps) that the quality unit's written procedures are followed in practice?
Are the quality unit's procedures reviewed and reapproved on a defined periodic cycle, with obsolete versions removed from circulation?
Does the quality unit have adequate laboratory facilities, or controlled access to qualified laboratories, available to it for testing and approval or rejection of materials and products?
Is the quality unit adequately staffed in number and qualification so that release and oversight decisions are not delayed or shortcut due to resource constraints?
Are the individuals performing quality unit functions qualified by documented education, training, and experience for the specific tasks they perform, including release and disposition?
Is there a current, controlled list or matrix of who is authorized to perform each quality unit function, including who may release and who may sign off on investigations?
Are release authority and other delegated quality decisions assigned in writing to named roles or individuals, with no informal or undocumented delegation?
When release authority is delegated during absences, is the delegation documented, time-bound, and limited to similarly qualified and authorized individuals?
Does the quality unit have defined authority over the review and approval of validation protocols and reports affecting product quality?
Does the quality unit have oversight of and approval authority over the change control system, so that changes affecting product quality cannot be implemented without quality approval?
Does the quality unit have defined authority over the disposition of returned, salvaged, reprocessed, and reworked product?
Is the quality unit responsible for the review and approval of supplier and contractor qualification decisions for materials and services affecting product quality?
Does the quality unit have a defined role in reviewing and approving the responses, root cause, and effectiveness checks for complaints and recalls?
Does the quality agreement with each contract facility specify that the contract giver's quality unit retains the ultimate authority to approve or reject product made on its behalf?
Is there a mechanism for the quality unit to escalate unresolved quality concerns or release disagreements to senior management, and is it documented when used?
Are conditional or quarantine-pending dispositions controlled so that product cannot be shipped before the quality unit grants final release?
Does the quality unit periodically review trends, such as recurring deviations or OOS results, and is that review documented and acted upon?
Is there documented evidence that management provides the quality unit the resources, access, and standing needed to fulfill its responsibilities without interference?
For products marketed in the EU, is the Qualified Person's batch certification role and authority defined and distinct from the US batch release function where both apply?

For cell and gene therapy or other products with short shelf life, does the quality unit have a defined, documented process for expedited release that still preserves full review and reject authority?
For sterile products, does the quality unit have explicit authority and procedures to reject batches based on sterility assurance, environmental monitoring excursions, and media fill failures?
For combination products, does the quality unit's authority extend across both the drug and device constituent parts, with disposition responsibility defined for the combined product?

Where computerized systems generate or hold data used for release decisions, does the quality unit confirm those systems are validated and the data reviewed (including audit trails) before relying on them for disposition?

sections.name: Document & Records Control
sections.intro: An investigator will test whether you control the lifecycle of every GxP document and record, from creation through approval, issuance, use, change, and retention, so that only current approved instructions are followed and every record is attributable, legible, contemporaneous, original, and accurate. Expect them to trace a few documents end to end, pull controlled copies at the point of use, check signatures and dates, and look for uncontrolled or obsolete versions in circulation.

questions.q: Do you have an approved procedure that defines your document hierarchy (for example policy, then SOP, then work instruction, form, and record) and explains how each tier relates to and governs the one below it?
Does every controlled document carry the required identifying metadata (unique document number, title, version or revision number, effective date, page x of y, and an owning function) on each page?

Is each GxP document reviewed and approved before use by the authoring function and by Quality, with the approvals captured as signatures and dates (or validated electronic equivalents) before the document becomes effective?

Are SOPs and other controlled documents reviewed and reapproved on a defined periodic cycle (for example every two or three years), and can you show that overdue periodic reviews are tracked and do not silently lapse?
At the actual point of use (production floor, lab bench, warehouse), is the only version of a document available the current approved one, with no obsolete or draft copies present?
When a document is revised, is the prior version promptly withdrawn from all points of use and either destroyed or clearly marked obsolete, so superseded instructions cannot be followed by mistake?
Do you maintain a master list or index of controlled documents that shows the current version, effective date, and status of each, and is it reconcilable against what is actually in circulation?
Are controlled paper copies issued through a defined mechanism (for example stamped, watermarked, or numbered) so that a controlled copy is visibly distinguishable from an uncontrolled printout?
Can you account for every controlled copy of a document that has been issued (issued-to and returned or destroyed), so that no controlled copy is unaccounted for when a document is superseded?

When users print from an electronic document system, is the printout automatically marked as uncontrolled or as valid only on the day of printing, so a stale printout cannot be mistaken for the current version?
Do your good documentation practices require entries to be made in indelible ink, legible, and recorded at the time the activity is performed rather than reconstructed afterward?

Are corrections to records made by a single line through the original entry (with the original remaining readable), the correct value entered, and the correction initialed and dated, with a reason recorded where it is not obvious (no overwriting, erasing, or correction fluid)?
Are blank spaces and unused fields on forms and records handled by a defined convention (for example crossed out, marked N/A, or zero-filled) so that no field is left open to later entry?
Is every record entry attributable to a specific person, with a defined and current signature and initials log that ties initials to full names and roles?
Do your records use an unambiguous, consistent date and time format, and where shift work or multiple time zones apply, is the convention defined so timestamps cannot be misread?
Are blank forms and master templates issued under control (for example logged, numbered, or reconciled) so that extra or unofficial blank forms cannot be created and used outside the system?
For batch records and other executed records, can you reconcile each issued copy against its return, so a record cannot go missing or be quietly replaced?
Do your good documentation practices explicitly prohibit pre-dating, post-dating, back-dating, and signing for work performed by another person?
Where electronic systems generate, modify, or store GxP records, do those systems enforce attributable, time-stamped, secure audit trails that cannot be altered or disabled by users?

For records kept electronically under 21 CFR Part 11 (or Annex 11), do you have validated systems, unique user accounts, no shared logins, and electronic signatures linked to their records so signatures cannot be excised, copied, or transferred?
Where a hybrid of paper and electronic records exists for the same activity, is it defined which one is the official raw data (the true record), and are both kept consistent and retained?

Do you have a defined records retention schedule that meets the applicable regulatory minimums (for example, batch and related records retained at least one year past the product expiration date per 21 CFR 211.180, with longer periods where required for biologics or other product types), and is the schedule followed in practice?
Are archived records stored in conditions that protect them from loss, damage, deterioration, fire, water, pests, and unauthorized access for the full retention period?
Are records retrievable within a reasonable time on request, with a defined indexing or retrieval method, so an investigator's request for a specific historical record can be met promptly?

For electronic records and the systems that read them, do you have a defined approach to preserve readability and access over the full retention period, including handling of legacy formats, system retirement, and data migration?
Are backups of electronic GxP records performed, verified, and periodically tested by restoration so you have confidence the records can actually be recovered?
Is destruction of records at end of retention controlled by procedure, authorized by Quality, documented, and prevented for any record under legal hold, investigation, or active litigation?
Is access to create, edit, approve, issue, and obsolete controlled documents restricted to authorized roles, with the document management system permissions reviewed periodically?
When a document change is made, is the change captured through a controlled change process that records what changed, why, the impact assessed, training needs, and the effective date?
Is training on a new or revised SOP completed and documented before, or by, the document's effective date, so personnel are not working to a version they have not been trained on?
Can you trace a single document end to end on demand (current version, prior versions, the change record for each revision, approvals, distribution list, and training records) without gaps?
Do documents that contain forms or templates control the form version in lockstep with the parent document, so an obsolete form cannot remain in use after the SOP is revised?

Are documents that originate with contract manufacturers, contract labs, or suppliers (for example certificates of analysis, batch records, validation reports) controlled, reconciled, and retained under your document control system per the quality agreement?
For records supporting product release and stability (batch records, analytical raw data, chromatograms, instrument printouts), is the original raw data retained, not just a summary or transcription?
Are laboratory notebooks, worksheets, and bound or paginated records controlled so that pages cannot be removed, inserted, or substituted without detection?

For sterile and aseptic operations, are environmental monitoring, media fill, and batch records controlled and contemporaneously completed at the line, rather than transcribed later from informal notes?
For cell and gene therapy and other biologics, are chain-of-identity and chain-of-custody records controlled, contemporaneous, and reconcilable for each patient-specific or donor-linked batch?

For combination products, do your document control and records cover both the drug and device constituent parts under the applicable cGMP streams per 21 CFR Part 4, including any device records (such as design and device history records) required for the device constituent so they are not left outside the system?

Are obsolete documents retained for the required period for traceability (to reconstruct what instruction was in force at the time a historical batch was made), separate from the active controlled set?

sections.name: Data Integrity & ALCOA+
sections.intro: An inspector in this area looks for a working data integrity program, not just a policy: GxP data that is attributable, legible, contemporaneous, original, and accurate across its full lifecycle, on paper and in computerized systems, backed by governance, risk-based controls tied to data criticality, and an independent second-person review that actually catches problems.

questions.q: Do you have an approved data integrity policy and governing procedures that define ALCOA+ (attributable, legible, contemporaneous, original, accurate, complete, consistent, enduring, available) and apply them to both paper and electronic records?

Has senior management formally established a data governance system with named accountability, a data integrity steering or oversight function, and evidence that DI is reviewed at the management level (for example in management review or quality council minutes)?

Have you performed a documented data integrity risk assessment that inventories your GxP systems and records, classifies data by criticality, and identifies where data could be deleted, altered, excluded, or fabricated?

Are controls (technical, procedural, and behavioral) assigned to each system or process in proportion to the criticality and risk of the data it holds, rather than applied as a one-size-fits-all standard?
Is every GxP record traceable to the unique individual who performed the action, with no shared logins, no generic accounts, and no unattributed entries on paper or in systems?

Are records made at the time the activity is performed (contemporaneous), and can you show that the time and date recorded reflect when the work actually happened rather than when it was later transcribed?

Do procedures prohibit pre-dating and back-dating, and is there a defined, documented method for recording a genuinely late or retrospective entry that clearly identifies it as such with the actual date of entry?

Are entries made directly onto the controlled record (not on scrap paper, sticky notes, gloves, or unofficial worksheets first), and does your DI policy explicitly forbid recording data on uncontrolled intermediate media?

When data is corrected on paper, is the original entry left legible with a single line through it, signed, dated, and given a reason for the change, with no use of correction fluid, overwriting, or obliteration?
Are controlled blank forms, logbooks, and worksheets issued, reconciled, and accounted for so that unofficial or duplicate blank forms cannot be used to re-record or selectively replace data?

Is the original record (or a verified true copy) retained for the full required retention period, and can you distinguish what your defined original record is for each data type, including printouts versus underlying electronic data?

Where true copies are made, is there a documented, verified process confirming the copy preserves the complete content and meaning of the original (including metadata and dynamic data) before any original is destroyed?

For dynamic electronic data (for example chromatograms, spectra, or reprocessable results), do you retain and review the electronic source data rather than relying only on a static PDF or paper printout?

Do your computerized GxP systems generate secure, computer-generated, time-stamped audit trails that capture who did what and when, including creation, modification, and deletion of data, and the original value before a change?
Are audit trails configured so they cannot be disabled, edited, or deleted by users, and is the audit trail function verified as part of system validation?

Is there a defined, risk-based audit trail review process that names who reviews which audit trails, at what frequency, and what they look for, with documented evidence the reviews are actually performed?
Is audit trail review for data supporting batch release or critical decisions performed before the data is approved or the batch is released, not only retrospectively?
Are system clocks for GxP computerized systems synchronized to a controlled time source and protected from user change so that timestamps are reliable?

Are user access rights assigned by role on the principle of least privilege, with administrator rights for GxP systems held by people independent of the data being generated (not the analysts or operators themselves)?
Is there a periodic, documented review of user accounts and privilege levels for GxP systems to confirm access still matches role and that leavers and role changes have been removed?

Do systems enforce unique user identification with controlled password rules, automatic logoff or session locking, and a defined lockout after failed attempts, so a record cannot be created or signed under another person's identity?

Are electronic signatures linked to their records so they cannot be copied or transferred, do they show the printed name, date and time, and meaning of the signing, and are signature-to-record links protected from change (21 CFR Part 11 / Annex 11)?
Is the use of test, demo, or training environments controlled so that no GxP decisions are ever based on data generated outside the validated production system or in an unqualified configuration?

Where standalone instruments or local data acquisition are used, is data saved to a secure, backed-up location promptly (not left only on a local drive or a USB stick), with controls preventing local deletion before saving?

Are aborted, trial, or unreported analytical runs and integrations controlled and accounted for, so there is no testing into compliance, unjustified reprocessing, or selective deletion of out-of-specification results?
Is data review designed as an independent second-person review by someone other than the person who generated the data, qualified to detect errors, omissions, and integrity problems?
Does the second-person review include the relevant raw data and metadata (not just the summary report), so that the reviewer could detect a missing or altered result?

Are review checklists or procedures specific enough that a reviewer knows exactly what to verify (for example calculations, transcriptions, integrations, audit trail entries) rather than only signing as a formality?

Is data transcription between systems or onto paper (manual transfer of results, weights, or volumes) verified by a second person or an electronic check to confirm it was transferred completely and accurately?
Are backups of GxP electronic data performed on a defined schedule, stored securely, and periodically test-restored to confirm the data and metadata are recoverable and intact?
Is GxP data archived in a way that keeps it accessible, readable, and complete (with metadata and audit trails) for the full retention period, including a plan for legacy or decommissioned systems?
Do you have a procedure and routine, documented practice for reporting suspected data integrity issues without fear of reprisal, and is there evidence the route is communicated and used?

When a data integrity event or discrepancy is found, is it investigated through your deviation and CAPA process, including an assessment of scope (whether other data or batches are affected) and root cause beyond blaming the individual?
Do your employees receive role-specific data integrity and good documentation practice training, with periodic refreshers, and can you show the training is understood rather than just attended?
Do quality agreements, audits, and oversight extend ALCOA+ and data integrity expectations to contract manufacturers, contract labs, and other suppliers who generate or hold your GxP data?

When you receive data from a vendor, CMO, or contract lab, do you have a defined way to assess its reliability (for example audit, raw data review, or system access) rather than accepting certificates of analysis or reports at face value?

Are hybrid systems (electronic record plus a paper signature or paper record plus an electronic system) formally defined, justified, and controlled so the linkage between the paper and electronic parts is unambiguous and complete?

For sterile or aseptic operations, are critical real-time records (environmental monitoring, line interventions, fill records) captured contemporaneously by personnel not solely relying on memory, so the record reflects what actually occurred at the time?

Do periodic self-inspections or internal audits specifically include data integrity behaviors and controls (for example checking for uncontrolled forms, audit trail review evidence, and shared logins), with findings tracked to closure?

sections.name: Computerized System Validation, Part 11 and Annex 11
sections.intro: An inspector probing computerized systems wants evidence that GxP systems are inventoried, risk-assessed, validated for their intended use, and kept in a validated state through change control and periodic review, with access tightly controlled to named individuals, electronic records protected and recoverable, and electronic signatures and audit trails meeting Part 11 and Annex 11. Expect them to pick a specific system and trace it end to end, from requirements through validation evidence, access list, audit trail review, backup proof, and supplier oversight.

questions.q: Do you maintain a current, controlled inventory of all computerized systems that lists each system's GxP status, the regulated process it supports, the data it holds, the owner, and its validation status?
Has each system on the inventory been assessed for GxP impact and risk so that the depth of validation and the controls applied are justified and documented rather than applied uniformly?

Are systems classified by a recognized scheme such as GAMP 5 software categories (for example category 1 infrastructure, 3 non-configured, 4 configured, 5 custom), with the classification driving the validation approach?
Do you apply a documented validation lifecycle (planning, requirements, specification, configuration/build, testing, release, and retirement) that is scaled to the risk and complexity of each system?
Is there an approved validation plan for each validated system that defines scope, responsibilities, the deliverables to be produced, and the acceptance criteria for release?

Are user requirements documented, uniquely identified, and traceable forward through specification and testing and back again, so you can show every requirement was tested and every test traces to a requirement?

Where you apply a Computer Software Assurance (CSA) risk-based approach, is the assurance effort and the mix of testing types (scripted, unscripted, exploratory) justified by the feature's patient-safety and data-integrity risk and recorded as evidence?

Do test records show the expected result, the actual result, the date, the tester, and objective evidence (such as screenshots or system output), with deviations during testing investigated and resolved before release?

Is each validated system formally released for GxP use through an approved validation summary or report that confirms acceptance criteria were met and lists any open items with their risk justification?
Do you leverage supplier documentation and audits to reduce duplicate testing only after assessing the supplier's quality maturity, and is that reliance documented rather than assumed?

Are infrastructure components (servers, operating systems, databases, networks, virtualized and cloud environments) qualified and kept under configuration control, with the cloud or hosting provider's responsibilities defined in an agreement?

Are all changes to validated systems (software, configuration, infrastructure, patches) managed through change control with impact and regression assessment, so that the system stays in a validated state?
Is every system access controlled so that each user has a unique individual login, with no shared, generic, or group accounts used to create, modify, or approve GxP records?
Are access rights assigned by defined role with least-privilege, approved before granting, and is administrator/privileged access restricted to people independent of the GxP data they could alter?
Do you perform and document periodic reviews of user access lists to confirm rights still match current roles and that leavers and role-changers have been removed or adjusted promptly?
Are password rules, account lockout, automatic session timeout/logout, and authority checks enforced by the system so that only authorized individuals can perform a given action?

For systems generating GxP electronic records, is a secure, computer-generated, time-stamped audit trail enabled that captures who did what and when for create, modify, and delete actions, including the original value where data is changed?
Are audit trails protected from being switched off or altered by ordinary users, and can you demonstrate that the audit trail cannot be disabled without leaving a record?
Do you perform routine, risk-based audit trail review (separate from full data review) on a defined frequency, with the review documented and any anomalies investigated?
Are system clocks synchronized to a controlled reference time source and protected from unauthorized change, so audit trail and record time stamps are trustworthy?
Where electronic signatures are used, do they include the printed name of the signer, the date and time, and the meaning of the signature (such as review, approval, or responsibility)?
Are electronic signatures linked to their records so that a signature cannot be copied, cut, or transferred to falsify another record?

Where electronic signatures use two distinct identification components such as user ID and password, does the system require all components for the first signing executed during a single continuous period of controlled system access, and at least one component for each subsequent signing within that period?

Has your organization submitted the one-time certification to FDA (per 21 CFR 11.100(c)) that electronic signatures used in your systems are intended to be the legally binding equivalent of handwritten signatures?
Can you reproduce GxP electronic records as accurate and complete copies in both human-readable and electronic form for the agency throughout the required retention period?
Are electronic records retained and protected so they remain readable and the meaning is preserved despite changes to software, hardware, or media over the retention period?

Where the system is a hybrid (electronic record with paper signature, or paper printout from an electronic source), is the link between the paper and electronic record defined and controlled so the complete record is preserved?
Are GxP data backed up on a defined schedule, with backups stored separately and protected, and is the integrity and completeness of backups verified?
Have you tested restoration from backup to prove that data and the system can actually be recovered, and is that restore test documented rather than assumed to work?
Do you have a documented and periodically tested business continuity and disaster recovery plan for systems whose loss would affect product quality, patient safety, or data integrity?
Are validated systems subject to documented periodic review that reconfirms validated status, current configuration, change history, deviations, access, and continued fitness for intended use?

Are cybersecurity controls (network segmentation, patching, antivirus/endpoint protection, vulnerability management, and removal of unsupported/end-of-life software) in place and assessed for systems holding GxP data?
Is there a documented process to detect, report, and investigate security incidents and unauthorized access affecting GxP systems, including the data integrity impact assessment?

When relying on software suppliers or cloud/SaaS providers, do you have current quality or service agreements that define responsibilities for validation support, change notification, data ownership, security, and access for inspection?
Are spreadsheets and end-user computing tools used for GxP decisions controlled, version-managed, and validated for their calculations rather than treated as informal tools?

When a validated system is decommissioned or replaced, do you have a documented retirement process that ensures records remain retrievable and readable for the full retention period and migrated data is verified?
Where data is migrated between systems or versions, is the migration planned, executed, and verified to confirm completeness, accuracy, and preservation of meaning and audit trail?
Are personnel who develop, maintain, use, or administer GxP computerized systems trained and qualified for their roles, with training records available to support competence?
For systems using artificial intelligence or machine learning in GxP decisions, are the models, training data, performance, and any retraining controlled and validated for the intended use?

sections.name: Audit Trails & Electronic Records Review
sections.intro: An inspector will test whether your GxP computerized systems capture a secure, time-stamped, who-what-when-why audit trail that cannot be turned off or altered, and whether you actually review those trails before the data is used to make a quality decision. Expect them to pick a specific high-criticality record, follow its full change history, and ask who reviewed the trail, when, against what risk basis, and what happened to the exceptions that surfaced.

questions.q: Do you maintain a current inventory of all GxP computerized systems that identifies, for each one, whether it has an audit trail, whether that trail is enabled, and the data criticality it supports?

For every system that creates, modifies, or deletes GxP electronic records, is a secure, computer-generated, time-stamped audit trail enabled and configured to capture operator entries and actions that create, modify, or delete those records (21 CFR Part 11.10(e))?
Does each audit trail entry capture who made the change, what was changed (including the original value and the new value), and when it occurred (date and time)?
Is the audit trail configured so that the original (first) entry is preserved and never overwritten when a value is later changed, leaving a visible chain from original to current value?
Have you verified during validation that the audit trail cannot be disabled, switched off, or bypassed by any user role, including administrators, during normal operation?
If a system technically allows the audit trail to be turned off, is that capability removed or locked down, and is any change to audit trail configuration itself recorded in a tamper-evident way?
Are users prevented from editing or deleting audit trail entries within the application?

For systems where data is stored in an underlying database or flat files, have you assessed and controlled the risk of records being changed directly at the database or operating-system level outside the application's audit trail?
Is the system clock protected from casual change, with the ability to adjust date and time restricted to authorized roles and any clock change recorded in an audit trail?
Are time zone and daylight saving handling defined and consistent across systems so that timestamps can be reconciled and the true sequence of events reconstructed during an investigation?
Are timestamps synchronized to a controlled, traceable time source (for example a network time server) so that events across integrated systems can be correlated reliably?
Do you have a documented, approved procedure that defines audit trail review: which systems and data are in scope, who reviews, how often, by what method, and how the review is recorded?
Is the scope and frequency of audit trail review driven by a documented data criticality and data risk assessment rather than by convenience or by what the tool happens to make easy?

For high-criticality data that feeds a quality decision (for example a QC release result), is the relevant audit trail reviewed as part of reviewing that record and before the data is approved or used, not only in a later periodic sweep?
Is the audit trail review performed by someone who understands the data and the process (the person responsible for the data or a qualified designate)?
Is the audit trail reviewer independent of the action being reviewed, so that the person who made or could benefit from a change is not the sole reviewer of that change?

When the review surfaces a meaningful event (an unexplained change, a deletion, a re-test, an aborted run, a time change, an out-of-sequence entry), is there a defined path to investigate it, document the outcome, and trigger a deviation or CAPA where warranted?

If you use review by exception, has the exception logic (the rules that pull entries forward for human review) been validated and demonstrated to reliably surface the events that matter against the complete data population?
For review by exception, is the exception review itself performed and documented, and can you show that the rules ran against the full set of entries rather than a filtered subset?
Is record-level audit trail review of high-criticality records kept tied to record approval, so review by exception does not replace examining that record's change history?

Is each completed audit trail review documented with the reviewer, date, system, records or period covered, the method used (manual or exception report ID and version), what was found, and what action was taken?
Can you pull any specific high-criticality record an inspector names and show evidence of where and when its audit trail was reviewed?

Are audit trail entries human-readable and convertible to an intelligible form (including for inspection), and have you confirmed that codes, abbreviations, or system IDs in the trail can be translated to plain meaning?
Are audit trails retained for at least the full record retention period of the data they describe?
When a system is upgraded, migrated, or retired, do you preserve access to historical audit trails (or a validated readable export) so the change history of legacy records is not lost?
Have you verified that a restore operation brings the data's audit trail back intact along with the records?
Do you capture and review log-on/log-off, failed access attempts, and changes to user accounts, roles, and privileges so that unauthorized access or shared-account use can be detected?
Are individual user accounts enforced (no shared or generic logins for GxP actions) so that every audit trail entry traces to one identifiable person?

For any standalone or simple system that lacks a built-in audit trail (for example certain instruments or spreadsheets), have you put a defined compensating control in place, such as a controlled second-person review of the raw data, and documented why it is adequate?

For hybrid records where the official record is part electronic and part paper, is it clearly defined which is the authoritative copy, and is the electronic audit trail reviewed alongside the paper rather than ignored?

Does the audit trail capture metadata needed to make the record meaningful (for example integration parameters, processing method, calculation, and reprocessing events for chromatography), not just the final reported value?
Are deletions and the reasons for them recorded, and do you specifically look for deleted, renamed, or aborted data files, injections, or runs that could indicate testing into compliance?
Is the audit trail review used to detect data that was generated but not reported (orphan or unattributed records), so that selective reporting of only passing results is caught?
Do periodic system-level audit trail reviews include time and clock change events, configuration changes, and administrator activity, with a defined risk-based frequency?
Have personnel who perform or oversee audit trail review been trained on what to look for and on the data integrity significance of the events they may see, with that training documented?

When a system is provided by a vendor or hosted by a third party, have you confirmed (through the supplier assessment or quality agreement) that the audit trail meets your requirements, is enabled, and that the vendor cannot silently alter records or trails?
For cloud or SaaS GxP systems, do you have access to the audit trail and a defined way to review and export it, rather than relying on the provider's assurance that one exists?

Are open-system controls (such as encryption or digital signatures where applicable) applied when records and their audit trails move across systems or are transmitted outside your control, to preserve authenticity and integrity?

sections.name: Deviation and Nonconformance Management
sections.intro: An inspector expects every unplanned departure from an approved procedure, specification, or expected result to be captured promptly, classified by risk, investigated to true root cause where warranted, assessed for product and batch impact, closed within defined timeframes, and rolled up into trending that catches recurring or systemic problems. Gaps in capture, late closures, weak impact assessments, and the absence of meaningful trending are among the most common findings cited under 21 CFR 211.192 and ICH Q10.

questions.q: Do you have an approved procedure that defines what a deviation or nonconformance is, the categories you use, and the steps and timeframes for capture, investigation, and closure?
Does your definition of a deviation cover departures from any approved procedure, specification, master batch record, or validated parameter, not only manufacturing process steps?
Are deviations recorded at or near the time of occurrence, with the date of discovery, date of occurrence, and the person who identified it captured, so that the timeline can be reconstructed?
Is there a clear mechanism for any employee to raise a deviation, and evidence that operators and floor staff actually initiate them rather than only quality or supervisors?
Can you demonstrate that deviations are not being downgraded to lesser tools such as logbook notes, comments, or planned-deviation entries to avoid the formal investigation process?
Do you classify each deviation by risk or severity using defined, objective criteria, for example critical, major, and minor, with documented rationale for the level assigned?
Does the classification drive the depth of investigation, the approval level required, and the closure timeframe, so that critical events get more rigor than minor ones?
Are classifications reviewed and confirmed by Quality, and is there a documented mechanism to re-classify upward if the investigation reveals greater impact than first thought?
Do you have defined target timeframes for each phase, such as initiation, investigation, and closure, and do you measure on-time performance against them?
When a deviation cannot be closed within its target timeframe, is a justified, approved extension documented before the due date rather than after, with a reason and a revised due date?
Can you show your current count and aging of open and overdue deviations, and is the backlog under control rather than growing?
Is every investigation scaled to the event, with the level of effort, the techniques used, and the people involved documented and matching the assigned risk classification?
For significant deviations, is a structured root cause analysis performed using a recognized method rather than defaulting to human error or an unverified assumed cause?
When human error is concluded as the root cause, are the underlying system factors (procedure clarity, training, workload) examined rather than only retraining the individual?
Is each investigation assessed for whether the same issue has occurred before or could affect other batches, products, equipment, or processes, and is that extent-of-condition review documented?
Does every deviation include a documented product quality impact assessment that addresses identity, strength, quality, purity, and safety as applicable, with a clear conclusion?

Is the impact assessment performed and approved by qualified personnel, and does it reference the data, specifications, or scientific rationale supporting the conclusion rather than a generic statement of no impact?
For any deviation associated with a batch, is the affected batch or batches specifically identified, and is the disposition of those batches linked to and dependent on the deviation conclusion?
Is there a hard control preventing release of an affected batch until the related deviation and its impact assessment are closed and approved by Quality?
When a deviation is discovered after a batch has already shipped, do you assess the need for a field action, complaint linkage, or recall, and is that decision documented?
Does the deviation process explicitly consider impact on validation status, stability commitments, and prior or concurrent batches, not only the batch in front of you?
Are deviations that warrant corrective or preventive action linked to your CAPA system, with clear criteria for when a CAPA is required versus a correction only?
Is the difference between an immediate correction or containment action and a true preventive action documented for each event, so corrections are not mistaken for root-cause fixes?
Before a deviation is closed, is there a check that all committed actions are complete or formally transferred to a tracked CAPA with a due date and owner?
Do you perform periodic trending of deviations by category, product, process, equipment, root cause, and area to detect recurring or systemic issues?
Does your trending look for repeat root causes and recurring events across separate deviations, and is a recurrence escalated even when each individual event was minor?
Are trending results reviewed by management at a defined frequency, documented, and used to drive systemic CAPA, with evidence the review actually leads to action?
Are planned (pre-approved) deviations approved before execution with a documented justification, defined scope, and expiry?
Are planned deviations limited in use and not relied on as a substitute for a permanent change through change control?
Is the deviation record traceable to the affected batch record, equipment, materials, analytical data, and any related events such as complaints, OOS results, or change controls?
Are out-of-specification and out-of-trend laboratory results handled through a defined investigation process that integrates with deviation and batch disposition decisions?
If deviations are managed in an electronic system, is it validated, with audit trails enabled and reviewed, and are records protected against unauthorized edits or backdating?

Are deviations involving contract manufacturers, contract labs, or suppliers captured, investigated, and reconciled under your quality agreement, with visibility into events that occur at the partner site?
For sterile or aseptic operations, are environmental excursions, media fill failures, and interventions captured as deviations with batch impact assessed against the affected fill?

For cell and gene therapy or other products with limited or single-patient batches, does the deviation process account for the inability to reject and rerun, with a documented benefit-risk path and prescriber or sponsor notification where applicable?
For combination products, do deviations cover both the drug and the device constituent parts, including device functionality and design-related nonconformances, under the appropriate quality system?
Are personnel who initiate, investigate, and approve deviations trained on the procedure and on root cause analysis techniques, with training records current?
Do your closed deviation records read consistently, so that the stated root cause, the impact conclusion, the actions taken, and the batch disposition all align without contradiction?

sections.name: CAPA & Effectiveness
sections.intro: An investigator will pull a sample of CAPA records and trace each one end to end: was the true root cause found, was the action actually corrective or preventive, did it close on time, was it proven effective, and did the problem recur. Expect scrutiny of the CAPA system as a whole (trending, escalation, backlog, links to deviations and complaints) and of individual records for quality, traceability, and honest effectiveness verification.

questions.q: Is there an approved CAPA procedure that defines the full lifecycle (initiation, investigation, root cause analysis, action planning, implementation, effectiveness verification, and closure) with defined roles and responsibilities?

Does the procedure define clear, risk-based criteria for when an event must escalate from a correction or deviation into a formal CAPA, so the decision is consistent and not left to individual judgment?

Do your records clearly distinguish among a correction (immediate fix or containment), a corrective action (eliminates the cause of an existing problem to prevent recurrence), and a preventive action (addresses a potential problem before it occurs), and apply each label correctly rather than defaulting everything to corrective?

Is a formal, documented root cause analysis performed for CAPAs using a recognized method (for example 5-Whys, fishbone/Ishikawa, fault tree), with the method appropriate to the complexity and risk of the problem?
Do investigations clearly separate the symptom from the true root cause, and avoid stopping at superficial causes such as human error without explaining why the error was possible?
When human error is cited as a root cause, is it examined for underlying systemic contributors (training, procedure clarity, workload, system design) rather than accepted as the default conclusion?
Does each corrective and preventive action logically address the identified root cause, so an auditor tracing the record can see a direct line from cause to action?

Is the scope of each investigation defined to assess whether the same root cause affects other products, batches, processes, equipment, or sites, rather than being limited to the single event that triggered it?
Are CAPA actions assigned to a named, accountable owner with a realistic and documented target due date based on the action and its risk?
Are due-date extensions controlled through a documented, justified, and approved process rather than being changed informally or repeatedly without rationale?
Do you track and trend the CAPA backlog and overdue CAPAs, and is the volume of overdue or aged records kept within defined limits with management visibility?
Is the risk of each problem assessed and documented, and is the depth of investigation and the urgency of action scaled to that risk?
For higher-risk CAPAs, is interim containment or risk mitigation put in place and documented while the permanent action is being implemented?
At the time of action planning, is an effectiveness check defined for the CAPA with its success criteria, verification method, data to be reviewed, and timing documented up front?

Are effectiveness checks based on objective, measurable evidence (for example defined acceptance limits, sample sizes, or monitoring data) rather than a subjective statement that the action was completed?
Is the effectiveness verification timing set to allow enough cycles, batches, or elapsed time to demonstrate the problem genuinely did not recur, instead of being checked the day the action closed?
Is there a clear distinction in your records between verifying that an action was implemented and verifying that the implemented action was effective, and are both performed?
If an effectiveness check fails, is there a defined path to reopen, escalate, or initiate a new CAPA, and can you show examples where this actually happened?
Is a CAPA prevented from being formally closed until its effectiveness has been verified (or a justified rationale documented where a delayed effectiveness check is appropriate)?
Do you track recurrence so that a repeat of the same or similar problem after a CAPA is detected, flagged, and triggers a review of whether the original root cause was correct?
Is CAPA data trended across deviations, complaints, OOS results, audits, and change controls to detect repeat issues, systemic problems, and patterns that individual records would miss?
Are the results of CAPA trending and the status of open, overdue, and ineffective CAPAs reviewed by management at a defined frequency, for example through the quality management review?
Are CAPA records cross-linked to their source records (deviation, complaint, OOS, audit finding, recall, change control) so the full history of an issue is traceable?
When a CAPA requires a change to a procedure, specification, equipment, or process, is that change driven through change control and is the linkage between the CAPA and the change documented?
Are personnel who perform root cause analysis and lead investigations trained in the methods and qualified for that role, with training records available?
Is the effectiveness review performed or approved by someone independent of the action owner, so the verification is objective rather than self-assessed?
Do CAPA records demonstrate ALCOA data integrity attributes (contemporaneous, attributable, complete, with supporting evidence attached)?
If your CAPA system is electronic, is it validated, access-controlled, and supported by an audit trail that captures who changed what and when, including status changes and due-date edits?
Are CAPAs originating from regulatory inspection observations, audit findings, and warning letters tracked in the same system with the same rigor, and can you show their root cause and effectiveness?
Do you avoid the common gap where multiple separate records share an obvious common cause but no single CAPA addresses the systemic issue, by aggregating related events where appropriate?
Are CAPA timeliness, quality, and effectiveness measured with defined metrics (for example percent on-time, percent effective on first check, recurrence rate), and are those metrics acted on?

For preventive actions specifically, can you show examples driven by proactive sources (trend analysis, risk assessments, near-misses, internal audits) rather than every action being reactive to a problem that already occurred?
When a product-impacting CAPA is identified, is the disposition of affected batches, the need for field action or recall, and notification to relevant quality functions assessed and documented?

For sterile or aseptic operations, do CAPAs arising from environmental monitoring excursions, media fill failures, or sterility assurance events receive root cause depth and effectiveness verification proportional to the patient risk?

For cell and gene therapy or other products with limited batches and long lead times, are effectiveness checks defined in a way that accounts for low manufacturing frequency rather than defaulting to a fixed number of consecutive batches that may take years to accrue?
For combination products, do CAPAs span both the drug and device aspects, and are CAPA requirements from both applicable quality systems reconciled so neither side is missed?
Is there a documented mechanism to share CAPA learnings across sites, departments, or product lines so that a fix applied in one area is evaluated and applied where the same risk exists elsewhere?

sections.name: Change Control
sections.intro: An investigator looks for a controlled, documented system that catches every change to a validated process, facility, equipment, material, method, system, or document before it happens, assesses its risk and regulatory impact, gets QA sign-off prior to implementation, and confirms the change worked without unintended effects. Expect them to trace specific changes end to end, from request through impact assessment, approval, implementation, post-change verification, and closure.

questions.q: Is there an approved written change control procedure that defines what counts as a change, when it must be raised, the steps from request to closure, and the roles and decision authorities at each step?

Does the procedure clearly distinguish change control from other quality systems (deviations, CAPA, planned vs unplanned events) so that planned changes go through change control and are not mislabeled as deviations after the fact?
Does the system require a change request to be raised and approved before the change is implemented, rather than documenting changes that have already been made?

Does each change request capture a clear description of the current state, the proposed change, the specific reason or justification, and the systems, products, processes, equipment, materials, methods, documents, or facilities affected?
Is every change uniquely numbered, logged in a central register or system, and traceable so that an investigator can pull the complete record for any change on request?
Does the system require a documented risk and impact assessment for every change before approval, scaled to the significance of the change?

Is there a defined, justified method for classifying changes by type or risk (for example minor, major, critical) that drives the depth of assessment, the approvals required, and the verification needed?
Does the impact assessment specifically evaluate effect on product quality, safety, efficacy, and on the validated or qualified status of the affected process, equipment, method, or system?
Does the impact assessment determine whether validation, qualification, or revalidation is required, and identify which specific protocols or studies must be executed?
Does the impact assessment evaluate regulatory and filing impact, including whether the change requires a regulatory submission, variation, or prior approval?

Are the right cross-functional subject matter experts (for example QA, Manufacturing, QC, Engineering, Regulatory, Validation, and where relevant Microbiology or Sterility Assurance) involved in assessing impact, with their input documented?

Does the change record evaluate impact on related documents (SOPs, batch records, specifications, labeling, training, validation master plan) and list the specific documents that must be created, revised, or retired?
Is QA approval of the change documented and obtained before the change is implemented, with the QA signature clearly distinguishable from technical or initiator approvals?
Does the record define the specific actions, owners, and due dates required to implement the change, including any pre-implementation conditions that must be met first?

Is there a documented verification, after the change is implemented, that all required actions were completed as approved and that the change was implemented as described, before the change is closed?
Does the system require defined acceptance criteria for post-change verification or effectiveness, so that closure is based on objective evidence rather than a simple sign-off?
When a change affects a validated state, is the required qualification or validation executed and accepted before the change is used in routine GMP operations or commercial product release?
Are interim or temporary changes controlled with a defined expiry or duration, a plan to revert or make permanent, and tracking so they do not silently become permanent unapproved practice?

Is there a defined mechanism to handle emergency or urgent changes that still requires risk assessment and QA authorization before or immediately at implementation, with full documentation completed promptly afterward?

Are change actions tracked to completion with target dates, and is there oversight of overdue actions and open changes (for example a periodic review or metric) so changes do not stay open indefinitely?
Is formal closure of a change performed only after all committed actions and required verification are complete, with QA confirming and documenting closure?

Are affected SOPs, batch records, specifications, and other controlled documents updated, approved, and effective before or at the moment the change takes effect at the point of use, with superseded versions removed?
Is required training on the change delivered and documented before affected personnel perform the changed activity?
Does the change control system feed product quality reviews and management review, so trends in change types, recurring changes, and overdue actions are visible and acted on?

Are changes proposed or implemented by contract manufacturers, testing labs, or suppliers controlled, with the quality agreement defining notification, approval, and the company's right to assess and approve before implementation?

For supplier or material changes (raw materials, components, primary packaging, excipients), is there a process to detect, assess, and approve changes that the supplier initiates, including changes to source, grade, or manufacturing site?
When a change requires a regulatory variation or submission, is implementation held until the appropriate notification or approval status is confirmed?

For computerized systems, does change control cover configuration, software, infrastructure, and interface changes, with assessment of validation impact, data integrity, electronic records and signatures, and security before implementation?

For computerized system changes, is there controlled migration from a qualified test or development environment to production, with regression testing scoped to the change and access to make production changes restricted and logged?

For sterile and aseptic operations, does change control specifically assess impact on sterility assurance, environmental control, aseptic process qualification or media fills, and the contamination control strategy before the change is implemented?

For biologics and cell and gene therapy products, does change control assess impact on comparability, the master and working cell or virus banks, critical reagents, and raw materials of biological origin, with a comparability plan where required?

For combination products, does change control evaluate impact across both the drug and device constituents, including device design, human factors, and the applicable design control and quality system requirements?

For APIs, does change control follow ICH Q7 expectations, including classification by potential impact on quality, evaluation of effect on the API specification and on downstream drug product, and notification of affected customers where required?

Does the system address the cumulative effect of multiple related or sequential changes to the same product, process, or system, so that incremental changes are not approved in isolation while their combined impact is missed?

When a post-change verification, validation, or stability result does not meet acceptance criteria, is there a defined path to halt or revert the change, raise a deviation or investigation, and prevent affected product from being released?

Is there a defined trigger to evaluate stability impact for changes that could affect product stability (for example formulation, process, packaging, or storage condition changes), and is stability committed where needed?

sections.name: Investigations, OOS & OOT
sections.intro: An investigator will test whether out-of-specification (OOS), out-of-trend (OOT), and other unexplained results trigger a timely, documented, scientifically sound investigation under 21 CFR 211.192, with disciplined Phase I laboratory assessment, Phase II full investigation reaching a supported root cause, defensible rules for retesting and resampling, and a hard look at whether the problem extends to other batches and products. Expect them to read closed investigations end to end and to challenge any invalidated result, averaging practice, batch release on a "passing" retest, and any conclusion of "lab error, no manufacturing impact."

questions.q: Do you have a written procedure for handling OOS results that aligns with 21 CFR 211.192 and the FDA OOS guidance, distinguishing a Phase I laboratory investigation from a Phase II full investigation with defined responsibilities and timelines?

Is the definition of an OOS result documented to include all results that fall outside specifications or acceptance criteria established in approved specifications, standards, or in-process limits, including in-process, stability, raw material, and finished product testing?

Does your procedure require that any unexplained discrepancy or failure of a batch or any of its components to meet specifications, whether or not the batch was already distributed, be thoroughly investigated under 211.192 with a written record?

Is every OOS result captured, logged, and investigated regardless of whether it occurs on a batch intended for release, a stability sample, a reserve sample, or a test repeated for any reason, so no failing result is informally discarded?

Are investigations initiated promptly when an OOS is detected, with a defined target completion time (commonly 30 days), and is any extension justified, documented, and approved rather than left open indefinitely?

In Phase I, does the analyst and supervisor assess laboratory aspects (such as method execution, calculation, equipment, reagents, standards, system suitability, and obvious error) before any retest, and is this assessment documented at the time it occurs?
Is an OOS result only invalidated when there is documented, scientifically justified, assignable laboratory cause for the error, rather than being invalidated on the basis of a passing retest alone?
Do you prohibit discarding the original OOS data and require that the original result, the investigation, and the rationale for any invalidation all be retained and traceable?
Does your procedure forbid 'testing into compliance,' meaning repeated retesting until a passing result is obtained without an identified assignable cause?

Are the rules for retesting predefined in the procedure, including who authorizes a retest, the number of retests, the sample used, and the criteria for accepting or rejecting the retest outcome, so retesting is not decided after the fact?

Is retesting performed only on a portion of the original sample (when scientifically valid) and is resampling allowed only when there is a documented, justified reason such as a demonstrated original sampling error, not simply because the first result failed?
Do you prohibit the use of outlier tests for chemical assays where they are not scientifically valid?
Does your procedure prohibit averaging a passing retest result with the original OOS result to obtain a reportable value?

When a clear, assignable laboratory root cause cannot be identified in Phase I, is the investigation escalated to a Phase II full-scale investigation that includes manufacturing, rather than being closed as probable lab error?

Does the Phase II investigation include a review of the manufacturing process, batch records, equipment, raw materials, in-process data, and previous batches to identify or rule out a manufacturing cause?

Is the quality unit responsible for evaluating and approving the conduct and conclusions of OOS investigations, and is the QA review documented before any decision to release, reject, or reprocess the batch?

Does each investigation reach a documented root cause (or a justified 'most probable cause' when a definitive cause cannot be established), and is a conclusion of 'no root cause identified, no impact' specifically challenged and supported rather than used as a default closure?

Is the impact of the OOS assessed across other batches, other products, and other lots that share the same materials, equipment, method, or time period, with a documented decision on whether the investigation must be extended?

When an investigation extends to other batches or already-distributed product, is there a documented evaluation of the need for field alerts, recalls, or notification to regulators, with the decision and its rationale recorded?
Are appropriate CAPAs identified from investigations, implemented, tracked to closure, and verified for effectiveness, rather than the investigation ending at root cause without corrective action?

Do you have a defined out-of-trend (OOT) program with statistical or rule-based criteria that flags results that are within specification but inconsistent with historical or expected behavior, for both stability and process data?

Are OOT results, including adverse stability trends, investigated and assessed for impact even when the individual result still meets specification, so a drifting attribute is caught before it becomes an OOS?
For stability studies, is a confirmed OOS or significant adverse trend evaluated for impact on the assigned expiry/retest date and on distributed batches, with notification considerations documented?

Is there a clear, documented distinction in your system between OOS, OOT, out-of-calibration, atypical or aberrant results, and laboratory incidents, so each type is handled under the correct procedure and none are mislabeled to avoid an OOS investigation?

Are investigation records complete and contemporaneous, capturing the original data, the analyst's account, the supervisor's assessment, hypotheses tested, results of retests, the conclusion, and all approvals with dates and signatures?

Does the investigation procedure require that any hypothesis for laboratory error (such as a dilution error or instrument malfunction) be confirmed by objective evidence wherever possible, rather than accepted as an unverified theory to justify invalidation?

Is batch disposition (release, reject, reprocess, rework) made only after the investigation is complete and approved by the quality unit, with a documented justification that addresses the OOS, and never on a conditional or pre-investigation basis?

Are investigations periodically trended (by product, method, instrument, analyst, error type, and root cause category) to detect recurring problems, systemic weaknesses, or a rising rate of invalidated results?
Is the rate of OOS invalidation reviewed by management, with an unusually high invalidation rate treated as a signal of either a lab quality problem or a procedure being used to dismiss true results?

For data captured in electronic systems (chromatography data systems, LIMS), does the system retain the original OOS result and full audit trail so an investigator can see the first result, any reprocessing, and any reintegration with documented justification?

Are reintegration of chromatograms and any reprocessing of electronic data performed only under a controlled procedure with documented scientific justification and second-person review, so manual integration cannot be used to convert an OOS into a passing result without a trail?
Are personnel who conduct or review investigations trained and qualified on the OOS/OOT procedure, statistics where applicable, and the regulatory expectations, with training records available?

When testing is performed at a contract laboratory or CDMO, do quality agreements define how OOS/OOT results are reported, investigated, and approved, and does your quality unit have oversight of and access to those investigation records?

For sterility test failures, do you invalidate the result only when an identified laboratory or test-system cause is documented, and otherwise treat the failure as a true result requiring full investigation?

For biologics and cell and gene therapy products with limited or single lots, perishable samples, or potency assays of high variability, does the procedure account for the scarcity of retest material and the assay variability so investigation decisions remain scientifically defensible rather than driven by sample availability?

Are confirmed OOS results on distributed finished drug products evaluated against your field alert reporting obligations under 21 CFR 314.81 within the required timeframe, with the assessment and any submission documented?
Does management review or quality metrics include open and overdue investigations, ensuring investigations are not closed prematurely to meet a timeline and that aging investigations are escalated?

sections.name: Laboratory Controls & Method Validation
sections.intro: An inspector in this area looks for laboratory controls that are scientifically sound, fully validated, and consistently followed: established specifications and sampling plans, test methods validated or verified for their intended use, system suitability that gates real results, controlled reference standards and reagents, and documented method transfer. Expect verification that the lab generates reliable, reproducible data and that any deviation from a method is justified, approved, and traceable.

questions.q: Are laboratory controls established in written procedures, including specifications, standards, sampling plans, and test procedures, and are they reviewed and approved by the quality unit as required by 211.160(a)?
