Are all specifications, standards, sampling plans, and test procedures scientifically sound and appropriate to assure that components, in-process materials, and finished products conform to identity, strength, quality, and purity, rather than carried over without justification?

Do acceptance specifications and limits have a documented scientific or statistical basis (for example tied to stability data, process capability, clinical relevance, or compendial requirements) rather than being arbitrary or default values?
Are sampling plans defined with a statistical or risk-based rationale that addresses the number of containers sampled, amount taken, and the points in the process where samples are drawn?

Does sampling describe how representative samples are obtained, including controls for stratified or homogeneity concerns and prevention of contamination or degradation of the sample during collection and storage?

Are calibration of laboratory instruments, apparatus, gauges, and recording devices done at suitable intervals per a written program, with documented accuracy and precision standards, and are instruments not meeting calibration tagged and removed from use per 211.160(b)(4)?
Is there a defined process to handle instruments found out of calibration, including an assessment of the impact on data generated since the last successful calibration?

Are test methods validated to demonstrate they are suitable for their intended use, with documented validation studies, before being used to release product or support stability decisions per 211.165(e)?

Do validation protocols and reports for quantitative methods address the relevant ICH Q2 characteristics for the method type (accuracy, precision including repeatability and intermediate precision, specificity, detection limit, quantitation limit, linearity, and range)?

Is specificity demonstrated so the method can distinguish and accurately quantify the target analyte even when impurities, degradation products, sample matrix, and excipients are present, supported by forced degradation or stress studies for stability-indicating methods?
Are the accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and reproducibility of test methods established and documented as required by 211.165(e)?

When a compendial (USP/NF or other pharmacopeial) method is used, is it verified under actual conditions of use to confirm suitability for the specific sample matrix, rather than assumed valid because it is compendial?

Is system suitability defined for chromatographic and other applicable methods with predefined acceptance criteria (for example resolution, tailing, theoretical plates, replicate injection precision, signal-to-noise), and must it pass before sample results are considered valid?

Is the practice of trial, test, or unofficial injections prohibited, and are system suitability and sample injections run as a single defined sequence so that no sample is injected before suitability is established and no results are selectively discarded?

Are reference standards (primary and secondary/working) qualified, characterized, and traceable, with documented identity, purity, assigned potency, and an assigned expiration or requalification date?
Are working or secondary standards qualified against a primary or compendial reference standard with a documented procedure, and is their continued suitability requalified on a defined schedule?
Are reference standards stored under defined conditions, inventory-controlled, and is there documented evidence that expired or unqualified standards cannot be used in testing?

Are reagents, solutions, volumetric standards, culture media, and mobile phases prepared per controlled procedures, labeled with preparation date, contents, concentration, preparer, and expiration or use-by date, and is their preparation traceable in records?
Are reagents and prepared solutions assigned scientifically justified expiration/use-by periods, and is there control to prevent use of expired reagents and standards?
Is incoming reagent and chemical quality controlled (grade, supplier, lot) and standardization performed and documented where required (for example titrants standardized before use)?

Is method transfer between laboratories (for example development to QC, or to a contract lab) governed by an approved protocol with predefined acceptance criteria, comparative or co-validation testing, and a report concluding the receiving lab can run the method reliably?
When method transfer is waived (transfer by verification, revalidation, or comparative testing exemption), is the rationale documented and justified rather than assumed?
Are all test data, including raw data, calculations, and electronic records, recorded contemporaneously, attributable to the analyst, and retained so results can be fully reconstructed per 211.194?
Is each reported result independently verified by a second qualified person or a validated system before it is reported?

For chromatographic data systems, are audit trails enabled, reviewed as part of result review, and is access controlled so that only authorized users can acquire, reprocess, or delete data, with reprocessing and reintegration justified and documented?
Are manual integration and method/parameter changes during data processing controlled, justified, and approved, rather than performed at the analyst's discretion to meet specifications?

Are out-of-specification (OOS) and out-of-trend (OOT) results investigated under a written procedure consistent with FDA OOS guidance, including a Phase I laboratory assessment before any retesting or invalidation?

Is invalidation of an original OOS result allowed only when an assignable laboratory cause is identified and documented, with retest and resample rules predefined rather than decided after seeing results?
Are stability test methods stability-indicating and validated to detect and quantify degradation products, and are stability samples tested per an approved protocol with results trended over time?

Are microbiological and compendial test methods (for example sterility, bioburden, endotoxin, antimicrobial effectiveness) validated or suitability-tested for the specific product matrix, including method suitability/growth promotion and inhibition controls?

For biologics and biological assays, are bioassays and potency methods validated with appropriate reference materials, system/assay suitability, and acceptance criteria that account for the higher variability of biological methods?

For cell and gene therapy products, are potency, identity, purity, and safety methods (for example vector copy number, viability, replication-competent virus, residual impurities) qualified or validated despite small lot sizes and short shelf life, with a risk-based strategy for evolving methods?

Is there control over revalidation, with defined triggers (method changes, instrument or column changes, transfer, failures, or process changes) and documented assessment of whether revalidation or partial revalidation is required?
Are standard and sample solution stabilities established so that the period for which prepared solutions can be used before reanalysis is supported by data?

Are laboratory test records reviewed and approved by the quality unit, and is there documented evidence that the quality unit reviews lab investigations, validation, and method changes before reliance on the data?
Is there control to ensure the current approved version of each test method is the only one available at the point of use, with obsolete method versions removed from the bench and data systems?
Are analysts trained and qualified on each method they perform, with documented competency, and is operator qualification reassessed when methods change?

Is laboratory glassware, columns, and consumables (for example HPLC columns) tracked and controlled so that method-critical consumables match what was used in validation, with column equivalency justified when substituted?

For combination products, are device-constituent and drug-constituent test methods both validated, including any methods unique to the device (for example delivered dose, extractables/leachables) under an appropriate testing strategy?

Are reserve and stability samples handled and tested separately from release samples, and is there assurance that data integrity controls prevent reuse of release data to satisfy stability obligations or vice versa?

sections.name: Stability Program
sections.intro: An inspector expects a written, science-based stability program that justifies every shelf life and storage condition with real data: protocols aligned to ICH Q1A-Q1F and Q5C, stability-indicating analytical methods, defined storage stations and pull points, an ongoing/commitment stability program covering marketed product, and active trending that feeds the quality system when results drift or fall out of specification. The self-checks below probe whether your program would hold up to that scrutiny.

questions.q: Do you have an approved written stability SOP/policy that defines protocol design, storage conditions, testing frequency, sample management, data review, trending, and the link to expiry/retest assignment, consistent with 21 CFR 211.166 and ICH Q1A?

Is every stability protocol approved before the first sample is placed on study, with the lots, conditions, time points, tests, methods, and acceptance criteria defined up front rather than reconstructed later?

Do your long-term, intermediate, and accelerated storage conditions match the ICH Q1A condition set appropriate to the product's intended climatic zone and storage label (for example 25C/60% RH long-term and 40C/75% RH accelerated for a Zone II room-temperature product)?
For products labeled for refrigerated storage (5C), do you run the long-term condition at 5C and an accelerated condition at 25C/60% RH per ICH Q1A?

For frozen or ultra-low-temperature products, have you defined and justified the storage conditions and an appropriate elevated stress condition, since standard accelerated conditions do not apply to frozen products?

Does your testing schedule follow the ICH Q1A frequency (for example 0, 3, 6, 9, 12, 18, 24 months then annually for long-term, and 0, 3, 6 months for accelerated), with any deviation from this scheme documented and justified?
Is your primary stability data based on at least three batches at the proposed commercial scale and process, as required to support a registered shelf life under ICH Q1A?
Where a significant change occurs at the accelerated condition, do you have a defined trigger and a procedure to add and test at the intermediate condition (for example 30C/65% RH)?
Are container-closure orientation requirements built into the protocol (upright and inverted/on-side) for liquid and semi-solid products where the product contacts the closure?
Do your stability protocols specify the actual marketed container-closure system and packaging configuration, rather than a surrogate or laboratory container?

For products with multiple strengths, container sizes, or fill volumes, have you applied bracketing or matrixing only where scientifically justified under ICH Q1D, with the design documented and approved?
Do your stability protocols include all stability-indicating attributes relevant to the specific dosage form (not assay alone), with the selected attribute set justified for that product?

Have your analytical methods been validated as stability-indicating, with demonstrated ability to separate and quantify the active ingredient from its degradation products, typically supported by forced-degradation/stress studies?

Do forced-degradation studies cover the relevant stress conditions (acid, base, oxidation, thermal, humidity, and photolytic exposure) and demonstrate mass balance and peak purity for the stability-indicating method?

Are degradation products that appear on stability identified, qualified, and controlled against ICH Q3B (drug product) limits, with reporting, identification, and qualification thresholds applied correctly?
Do you assess nitrosamine and other process- or formulation-related impurity risks over the shelf life where applicable, consistent with current ICH M7 and regulatory expectations?
Is photostability testing per ICH Q1B performed on at least one primary batch, covering both the fully exposed product and, where relevant, the product in its immediate and marketing pack?
Are stability chambers qualified and on a calibration/preventive-maintenance schedule, with continuous temperature and humidity monitoring, alarms, and a documented response procedure for excursions?
When a chamber excursion occurs, do you assess and document the impact on samples (cumulative time out of condition, whether to continue, repull, or invalidate) rather than silently resuming?
Is there clear chain-of-custody and inventory control for stability samples, so you can show that the correct lot/condition/time-point sample was pulled, tested on schedule, and reconciled?
Are stability time points pulled and tested within the defined window (for example plus or minus a stated number of days), with out-of-window pulls documented and assessed?

Is your assigned expiration date (drug product) and retest period (drug substance) supported by statistical evaluation of long-term data per ICH Q1E, rather than chosen arbitrarily or copied from a similar product?

Where you extrapolate shelf life beyond the available long-term data, is the extrapolation justified by ICH Q1E criteria (statistical analysis, no significant change at accelerated, and supporting data), and is it limited to what the data support?

Do you have an ongoing (commitment) stability program that places at least one commercial batch per year per product, strength, and container-closure system on long-term study (a written stability program is required by 21 CFR 211.166, with the per-year enrollment cadence per ICH Q1A)?

Are batches enrolled in the ongoing program selected and placed within a defined timeframe after manufacture, with a documented rationale if fewer than one batch per year is enrolled (for example no batch produced that year)?
Do you place batches on stability following any significant change (formulation, process, site, supplier, container-closure) to confirm the existing shelf life still applies?

Is biologic/biotechnological product stability designed per ICH Q5C, addressing potency/bioactivity, aggregation and fragmentation, charge variants, and other product-specific quality attributes over the shelf life?

For cell and gene therapy products, does the stability program address attributes such as viability, viable cell count, potency, vector titer/identity, and sterility, with conditions reflecting the short or cryopreserved shelf life and the actual storage and shipping path?

Are in-use, in-transit, and post-reconstitution/post-thaw stability studies performed where the product is diluted, reconstituted, thawed, or held before administration, with the resulting hold times reflected on the label?
Does your stability program support the labeled excursion/transport conditions and any mean-kinetic-temperature claims, rather than only the steady-state storage condition?
Is each stability result reviewed against its acceptance criterion on a defined timeline, and are out-of-specification stability results promptly entered into the OOS/investigation process?

When a confirmed stability OOS or an emerging adverse trend implicates marketed product, do you have a procedure to assess shelf-life impact, notify the relevant authority, and evaluate the need for field action or recall within required timeframes?
Do you perform proactive trend analysis of stability data across time points and batches (not just pass/fail at each pull) to detect drift toward a limit before an OOS occurs?
Are statistically- or scientifically-derived alert and action limits used for stability trending, with a defined response when a trend breaches them?
Are stability conclusions and any shelf-life changes captured in the Annual Product Review/Product Quality Review, with the ongoing-stability status reported each period?
Is stability raw data (chromatograms, audit trails, chamber records, calculations) retained, attributable, and ALCOA+ compliant, so a reviewer can reconstruct each reported result from source?
When stability testing is outsourced to a contract laboratory, do you review and approve their protocols, methods, data, and OOS investigations under a governing quality/technical agreement?

Are stability samples stored and tested using the same validated methods and qualified instruments as release testing, with method changes assessed for impact on ongoing studies and historical comparability?
Do you have a documented procedure for handling samples and data when a study is terminated early or a protocol is amended mid-study, preserving the integrity and traceability of the original design?

sections.name: Process Validation Lifecycle (PPQ & CPV)
sections.intro: An inspector here is testing whether process validation is run as a connected lifecycle, from process design through process qualification (including PPQ) into ongoing continued process verification, rather than a one-time event. Expect them to trace specific products through Stage 1 design rationale, Stage 2 PPQ protocols and acceptance criteria, and Stage 3 monitoring with real statistical evidence, and to check that 21 CFR 211.100 and 211.110 controls are actually in place at the point of manufacture.

questions.q: Is there a written process validation policy or master plan that defines the three-stage lifecycle (process design, process qualification, continued process verification) and states how it satisfies 21 CFR 211.100 and the current FDA process validation guidance?
For each commercial product, can you produce a current validation status summary (a validation master plan or matrix) that shows which stage each process is in and what is outstanding?

Does the Stage 1 process design documentation capture the source knowledge, including development data, prior platform knowledge, design of experiments, and the rationale for the chosen process parameters and ranges?
Are critical quality attributes (CQAs) defined for each product with a documented justification linking them to patient safety, efficacy, and product quality?
Are critical process parameters (CPPs) identified and distinguished from non-critical parameters, with a documented risk assessment (for example FMEA) tying each CPP to the CQAs it affects?
Is there a documented control strategy that links CQAs, CPPs, material attributes, in-process controls, and release testing, so it is clear how each attribute is controlled across the process?
Were proven acceptable ranges and normal operating ranges established from data rather than set arbitrarily, and is the basis for each range traceable?

Before PPQ began, was facility, utility, and equipment qualification (IQ/OQ/PQ) complete and documented, including qualification of the supporting utilities (water, HVAC, compressed gases) that contact or affect the product?
Were the analytical methods used to test PPQ samples validated or qualified, and is that validation status documented and current before the methods were used to judge PPQ results?
Was a PPQ protocol written and approved by quality and the relevant technical functions before the PPQ batches were manufactured, rather than reconstructed afterward?
Does the PPQ protocol state pre-defined, quantitative acceptance criteria for CQAs, CPPs, in-process results, and yield, rather than relying only on routine release specifications?
Is the number of PPQ batches justified by a documented rationale based on process risk, complexity, and prior knowledge, rather than defaulting to a fixed number of three without explanation?

Does the PPQ protocol define the enhanced sampling and testing plan (location, frequency, and number of samples) used to demonstrate intra-batch and inter-batch consistency beyond normal routine controls?
Were PPQ batches manufactured under routine commercial conditions using commercial-scale equipment, the intended manufacturing site, trained production staff, and approved master batch records?
Is there documented evidence that the personnel who executed PPQ were trained and qualified on the specific process and procedures involved?
Were all deviations, atypical events, and out-of-specification or out-of-trend results during PPQ investigated and dispositioned before the process was declared validated?
Does the PPQ summary report present the actual data against each pre-defined acceptance criterion, with a documented conclusion approved by quality, rather than a generic pass statement?
If any PPQ acceptance criterion was not met, was the result investigated under the deviation/OOS process and a quality-approved disposition reached before the process was declared qualified?
Was commercial distribution of PPQ batches authorized only after the PPQ report was reviewed and approved by the quality unit?
Has a continued process verification (Stage 3) program been established and approved that defines what parameters and attributes are monitored, at what frequency, and by what statistical methods?

Are CPPs and CQAs trended over time using appropriate statistical tools (for example control charts and capability indices such as Cpk/Ppk), rather than only checking each batch against specification?
Are statistically based control limits or alert and action limits defined for the monitored parameters, and are they distinct from the registered specification limits?
When a parameter trends toward an alert or action limit or shows an out-of-trend signal, is there a documented procedure that triggers investigation before a specification failure occurs?
Is the continued process verification data reviewed at a defined frequency by a cross-functional team, with the review documented and signed?
Does the continued process verification program evaluate process variability and detect drift or shifts over time, and is there evidence the process remains in a state of control?

Are findings from continued process verification fed into the annual product review or product quality review, and do those reviews assess process performance and the adequacy of current specifications?
When monitoring shows the process is not performing as validated, is there a documented mechanism to trigger CAPA, process improvement, or revalidation?
Are criteria for revalidation or re-qualification defined (for example significant process, equipment, formulation, site, or supplier changes), and are they enforced through change control?
Are process and equipment changes that could affect the validated state assessed through change control, with a documented evaluation of revalidation needs before implementation?
Does each batch production and control record establish, per 21 CFR 211.100(b), that production controls are followed and documented at the time of performance?

Per 21 CFR 211.110(a), are in-process control procedures established to monitor output and validate performance of the manufacturing steps that may cause variability in the finished product, with appropriate in-process sampling and testing?

Are valid in-process specifications consistent with final product specifications and derived from previous acceptable process average and process variability estimates, as required by 21 CFR 211.110(b)?
Is statistical sampling and the statistical rationale for in-process and release sampling plans documented and based on sound statistical principles rather than convenience?
For cleaning processes that affect product quality, is cleaning validation established with documented acceptance limits and is its ongoing verification linked to the process validation lifecycle?

For sterile or aseptically processed products, is process qualification supported by current aseptic process simulations (media fills) and is that qualification kept current within the defined re-qualification interval?

For biologics and cell and gene therapy products, does process validation address sources of biological variability such as raw material and cell-bank variability, viral or impurity clearance, and lot-to-lot consistency of the active substance?
For continuous manufacturing or processes using real-time release testing or PAT, is the model maintenance, real-time monitoring strategy, and handling of diverted material defined and validated?

When validation activities or sample testing are performed by a contract manufacturer or contract lab, does the quality agreement assign responsibility for protocols, acceptance criteria, data, and continued process verification, and does your firm review and approve that work?

Are process validation protocols, reports, raw data, and electronic records retained, controlled, and attributable in line with data integrity expectations, so an inspector can trace a reported PPQ or CPV value back to its source data?

sections.name: Cleaning Validation
sections.intro: An inspector here is testing whether your cleaning is scientifically justified and proven, not just performed: defensible acceptance limits tied to toxicology and dose, validated sampling and recovery, a documented worst-case rationale, controlled hold times, and a sound visual-inspection program, all under a written program with a current validated state and change control.

questions.q: Is there a written cleaning validation master plan or policy that defines your overall approach, the lifecycle stages (design, qualification, ongoing verification), responsibilities, and the criteria for when full validation versus verification applies?

Are cleaning procedures (SOPs or batch-record steps) detailed enough that a different operator on a different shift would clean the same way, specifying agent, concentration, temperature, contact time, rinse volume, mechanical action, and any disassembly?
Are maximum allowable carryover (MACO) acceptance limits established for each product-to-product changeover using a documented scientific rationale rather than an arbitrary default?
Where you set acceptance limits, do you calculate them using a health-based exposure approach (PDE or ADE) for the active, and not solely the older 10 ppm or 1/1000 of therapeutic dose conventions?

Is each PDE or ADE value documented in a toxicological assessment authored or reviewed by a qualified toxicologist, with the data, point of departure, and adjustment factors recorded and kept current?
When more than one limit calculation method is available, do you apply the most stringent (lowest) resulting limit as your acceptance criterion?

Do your MACO calculations correctly use the shared equipment surface area, the minimum batch size of the next product, the largest daily dose of the next product, and the smallest (minimum) therapeutic dose of the previously manufactured product?
Is the total product-contact surface area of each piece of shared equipment and of each equipment train documented and verified against engineering drawings rather than estimated?
Are cleaning agent and detergent residues themselves limited and tested, with limits based on the toxicity of the cleaning agent and removal verified?
Is microbial and, where relevant, endotoxin contamination addressed in your cleaning acceptance criteria, not just chemical residue of the active?
Are sampling locations chosen and justified as the hardest-to-clean points (worst-case locations), and documented with diagrams or photos so the same points are sampled every time?
Where you use swab sampling, is the swab technique, swab material, swabbed area, and solvent defined, and have operators been trained and their swabbing technique qualified?
Where you use rinse sampling, do you justify it (for example for hard-to-reach surfaces) and recognize its limitation that it measures only what dissolves into the rinse, not adhered residue?
Has a recovery study been performed for each residue, surface material of construction, and sampling method, and is the recovery factor applied to your results?
Do your recovery results meet a pre-defined acceptance criterion, and if recovery is low, do you have a documented justification or a correction factor rather than ignoring the loss?
Are the analytical methods used for residue testing validated for the intended use, including specificity for the target residue and a quantitation limit below the acceptance limit?
When using total organic carbon (TOC) as a non-specific method, is its use justified, and do you understand and document what it does and does not capture relative to a specific method?

Have you defined and justified a worst-case product within each product group or family (for example based on solubility, toxicity or PDE, dose, and difficulty of cleaning) so that validating it represents the group?

Is your equipment grouping or bracketing approach documented with the rationale for why grouped equipment is equivalent (same design, materials, and cleaning procedure), and is new equipment assessed against existing groups?
Has cleaning validation been performed on a defined number of consecutive successful runs (typically three) for each worst-case scenario, with the rationale for the number of runs documented?
Is the dirty hold time (the maximum time equipment may sit soiled before cleaning) established and validated, and enforced in operations?
Is the clean hold time (the maximum time clean equipment may be stored before use or before requiring re-cleaning) established and validated, including microbial growth over that period?
Are dirty and clean hold times recorded for each batch (cleaning end time, storage, next use) so adherence can be verified during an inspection?
Do you have a defined and justified equipment campaign length and re-cleaning frequency, with validation supporting cleaning after the maximum campaign?
Is visual inspection for cleanliness a documented requirement after cleaning, performed under defined lighting and viewing distance, by trained and qualified inspectors?

Have you established the visual detection limit (the level at which residue becomes visible) and confirmed it is below or consistent with your calculated chemical acceptance limit before relying on visual checks?
Is visual inspection used as a complement to analytical testing rather than as the sole release criterion where quantitative limits are required?

For automated clean-in-place (CIP) or wash-in-place systems, are critical cleaning parameters (flow, pressure, temperature, conductivity, time) monitored, recorded, and within validated ranges for each cycle?

Is dedicated (non-shared) equipment used for products where carryover cannot be adequately controlled by cleaning, such as beta-lactams, highly sensitizing materials, and certain highly potent compounds, and is that decision documented with a risk-based rationale?
For shared facilities and equipment, do you have a documented cross-contamination risk assessment (such as a quality risk management exercise) that justifies shared use and the controls applied?

Is there ongoing cleaning verification or periodic monitoring after initial validation (for example routine sampling, trending, or periodic re-qualification) to confirm the cleaning process stays in a validated state?
Are cleaning validation results, deviations, and trends reviewed under a defined program, with out-of-limit results investigated and addressed through CAPA?
Does change control capture changes to formulations, equipment, cleaning agents, batch sizes, or procedures, and trigger an assessment of impact on cleaning validation and limits?
When a new product is introduced, is it assessed against the existing worst-case and limits before manufacture to confirm the current validation still covers it or to trigger revalidation?

For biologics and cell and gene therapy products, does your cleaning approach address degradation of the active during cleaning, removal of process residues and bioburden, and the use of inactivation or denaturation rather than relying on intact-molecule limits where appropriate?

For biologics, do you justify acceptance limits in terms of degraded or denatured protein and process-related impurities rather than applying a small-molecule PDE that may not be meaningful for a large molecule?

For cell and gene therapy operations, are single-use or disposable contact surfaces controlled and qualified, and is cleaning validation scoped only to the genuinely reused, shared product-contact equipment?

For sterile manufacturing, is equipment cleaning integrated with the sterilization or sanitization program so that cleaning validation, depyrogenation where needed, and bioburden control are consistent and not in conflict?

For API manufacturing, are cleaning limits and worst-case justifications consistent with ICH Q7 expectations, including handling of intermediates, recovered solvents, and equipment used across multiple API steps?

Are cleaning records (cleaning logs, equipment status labels or tags, and use logs) maintained so that the clean or dirty status and the last cleaning event of any equipment can be reconstructed at any time?

sections.name: Equipment & Instrument Qualification, Calibration, Maintenance
sections.intro: An investigator in this area expects to see that equipment and instruments used in manufacturing and testing are of appropriate design, qualified for their intended use through a documented lifecycle (DQ/IQ/OQ/PQ scaled by risk and analytical instrument category per USP <1058>), kept calibrated against traceable standards with defined limits and out-of-tolerance handling, and maintained on a planned, recorded schedule. Records should demonstrate the equipment was fit for use at the moment each batch was made or each test was run.

questions.q: Do you maintain a current, complete inventory or asset register of all GxP equipment and instruments that identifies each unit by unique ID, location, qualification status, and calibration/maintenance status?

Can you show a documented, risk-based rationale for the level of qualification effort applied to each piece of equipment and instrument (for example, why a given item received full DQ/IQ/OQ/PQ versus a reduced approach)?

Is Design Qualification (DQ) documented for new or significantly modified equipment, demonstrating that the selected design meets defined user requirements (URS) and intended use before purchase or construction?
Do qualification protocols (IQ/OQ/PQ) define acceptance criteria before execution, and are protocols formally reviewed and approved by Quality prior to execution?

Does Installation Qualification (IQ) verify correct installation against manufacturer specifications, including utilities, software/firmware versions, components, materials of construction, and that required documentation (manuals, P&IDs, spare parts lists) is present?

Does Operational Qualification (OQ) challenge the equipment across its intended operating ranges, including alarms, interlocks, and worst-case operating limits, with documented results against pre-set criteria?
Does Performance Qualification (PQ) demonstrate consistent performance under actual or simulated production conditions using the intended process, loads, and materials over a defined number of runs?
Are qualification deviations, discrepancies, and out-of-specification results during IQ/OQ/PQ documented, investigated, and resolved before the equipment is released for GxP use?

Is there a formal qualification summary report for each system that references all protocols, documents that acceptance criteria were met, and provides a clear release-for-use statement approved by Quality?

Are analytical instruments classified by group (for example Group A, B, or C per USP <1058>), with the assigned classification driving the level of qualification and ongoing verification applied to each instrument?

For Group C analytical instruments (those requiring full qualification, such as HPLC, GC, dissolution, spectrophotometers), is DQ/IQ/OQ/PQ documented and is operational verification repeated at defined intervals or after major changes?

Is there a documented program ensuring measuring and recording devices have suitable accuracy and precision for their intended use, as required by 21 CFR 211.68 and 211.160(b)(4), before they are relied upon for decisions?
Do you have a written calibration program (SOP) that defines which instruments require calibration, the frequency, the methods, the acceptance limits, and the standards to be used?

Are calibration intervals defined for each instrument with a documented basis (manufacturer recommendation, criticality, historical drift), and is the program followed without unexplained overdue items?

Are calibration tolerance/acceptance limits defined for each instrument and set appropriately tight relative to the process or test requirement the instrument supports (for example, tolerance narrower than the specification it controls)?
Are calibration standards traceable to a recognized national or international standard (for example NIST), and do you retain current traceability certificates for the reference standards in use?
Does each calibrated instrument carry a current calibration status label (or equivalent system indicator) showing it was calibrated, by whom, the date done, and the date due?
Is there a defined, documented procedure for handling out-of-tolerance (OOT) calibration findings, including impact assessment on product and data generated since the last successful calibration?

When an instrument is found out of tolerance, do you perform and document a retrospective impact ("look-back") evaluation of all batches or test results generated by that instrument since it was last known to be in tolerance, with disposition decisions?
Are as-found and as-left calibration readings both recorded, so that the magnitude of any drift and any adjustment made can be evaluated?
Are instruments that are out of calibration, defective, or not yet qualified clearly identified and removed from service or quarantined to prevent inadvertent GxP use?
Do you have a written preventive maintenance (PM) program with defined tasks and frequencies for each piece of equipment, derived from manufacturer recommendations and use history?
Are preventive maintenance activities scheduled, tracked, and completed on time, with records showing what was done, by whom, when, and the parts/lubricants used?

Are lubricants, coolants, and other maintenance materials that contact product (or could contact product) controlled so they do not alter the safety, identity, strength, quality, or purity of the product beyond established requirements, per 211.65(b)?
Is unplanned (breakdown/corrective) maintenance documented, and is an assessment made of whether the failure could have affected product quality or data integrity since the last verified-good state?
After maintenance or repair that could affect performance, is the equipment re-qualified, re-calibrated, or re-verified as appropriate before being returned to GxP service?
Is equipment cleaning addressed for product-contact equipment, with cleaning procedures, validation/verification, and use/cleaning logs maintained per 211.67, and is cleaned status identifiable?
Are equipment use logs (or an equivalent record) maintained showing the product, batch, date, and the cleaning and maintenance performed for each use, per 211.182?

Are changes to qualified equipment (hardware, software/firmware, configuration, location, operating range) routed through change control with an assessment of the need for re-qualification or re-validation before the change goes live?

Is equipment designed, sized, and located to facilitate operation, cleaning, and maintenance, and constructed so that surfaces contacting components or product are non-reactive, non-additive, and non-absorptive, per 211.63 and 211.65?

Is automatic, mechanical, and electronic equipment used in production or testing routinely calibrated, inspected, or checked according to a written program, with those checks documented, per 211.68(a)?
For computerized or automated equipment, are appropriate controls in place over changes to programs, with these controls documented per 21 CFR 211.68(b)?

For equipment that records or stores GxP data, are electronic records and audit trails reviewed and protected so that calibration, qualification, and run data cannot be altered without traceability (ALCOA+)?

Are temperature-mapping and load-pattern qualifications performed and periodically re-verified for thermal equipment such as autoclaves, ovens, lyophilizers, incubators, and cold-storage units, covering worst-case load configurations?

For sterilization and depyrogenation equipment, do qualification studies establish and document the critical process parameters (for example F0, exposure time, temperature distribution) and is biological/endotoxin challenge included where applicable?

For single-use systems and disposable equipment used in biologics or cell and gene therapy manufacturing, is there a documented qualification/verification approach (extractables/leachables, integrity, supplier qualification) appropriate to their intended use?
Is there a documented program for periodic review or requalification of equipment and systems at defined intervals to confirm they remain in a qualified, validated state over their lifecycle?

Are personnel who perform qualification, calibration, and maintenance trained and qualified for those tasks, and are external service providers/contractors qualified with their work reviewed and approved by your Quality unit?
Can you readily retrieve, for any given batch or test result, the qualification status, calibration status, and maintenance status of every piece of equipment used at that point in time?

sections.name: Facilities, Utilities & Environment
sections.intro: An inspector here is checking that buildings, utilities, and the controlled environment are designed, qualified, monitored, and maintained so they cannot adulterate product, cause mix-ups, or introduce contamination. Expect scrutiny of facility layout and flows, HVAC qualification, water and pure-steam systems, compressed gases, environmental and utility monitoring, pest control, and the maintenance and change control that keep all of it in a validated state.

questions.q: Is the facility designed with defined flows for personnel, materials, product, waste, and equipment that prevent cross-contamination and mix-ups, with those flows documented in a current layout drawing that matches the as-built facility?

Are unidirectional flows enforced where required (for example clean-to-dirty separation, no backtracking of soiled items through clean areas), and can you show the controls (interlocks, procedures, signage) that maintain them?

Are operations of incompatible products or activities (for example different live products, potent compounds, viral and non-viral steps) segregated by dedicated areas, closed systems, or validated time-and-space campaign controls with documented changeover and cleaning?

Are areas classified to a defined cleanliness grade (for example ISO 5/7/8 or EU Grade A/B/C/D), with the classification documented, posted or controlled, and matched to the activities performed in each room?
Is room cleanliness qualified at rest and in operation, and do you have current data showing each classified area meets its non-viable particle limits in both states?
Are room pressure differentials defined, continuously monitored, alarmed, and recorded, with airflow direction (cascades from cleaner to less clean) verified and documented?
Are airlocks, gowning rooms, and pass-throughs designed and used so that interlocks or procedural controls prevent simultaneous opening of doors between differently classified areas?

Are interior surfaces (walls, floors, ceilings, coving) of manufacturing and classified areas smooth, non-shedding, cleanable, and free of cracks, peeling, or unsealed penetrations, with this verified during routine facility inspections?

Is the HVAC system qualified (IQ/OQ/PQ) for the areas it serves, with documented evidence that air changes per hour, recovery times, temperature, humidity, and filtration meet the design intent for each classified space?

Are HEPA filters in classified areas integrity tested on a defined schedule (for example annually or after maintenance) with current passing results, and are filter locations and test dates traceable?

Are airflow visualization (smoke) studies available for Grade A / ISO 5 critical zones, performed in the as-operated state, and do they demonstrate unidirectional airflow over exposed product and components?
Is HVAC re-qualified on a periodic basis and after significant changes, and is there documented justification for the requalification frequency and scope?

Are room temperature and humidity limits defined for each area based on product and operational needs, continuously or routinely monitored, alarmed, and is monitoring data reviewed with deviations investigated?

Is the pharmaceutical water system designed, constructed, and qualified for its intended grade (Purified Water, Water for Injection, or other) following USP <1231> principles, with materials of construction, slopes, and dead-leg controls documented?

Is the water system maintained in a continuously circulating, self-sanitizing condition where required (for example hot WFI loops or controlled circulation), and are sanitization cycles defined, performed, and recorded?

Do you have a documented water sampling plan covering every use point and the loop on a defined frequency, with chemical (conductivity, TOC) and microbial testing including endotoxin for WFI, against established specifications?

Are alert and action levels for water chemical and microbial attributes defined, trended over time, and are excursions investigated with documented impact assessment to product already released or in process?

Was the water system qualified through a recognized multi-phase performance qualification approach (an initial intensive sampling phase, a continued sampling phase, and a long-term phase covering a full year to capture seasonal variation) before routine use, and is a current periodic review of system performance maintained?

If Water for Injection or pure steam is produced, is the still or generation method qualified, and is pure steam quality (non-condensable gases, dryness, superheat) tested where it contacts product or product-contact surfaces?

Are compressed gases and process air that contact product or product-contact surfaces tested against defined specifications for the relevant attributes (identity, purity, moisture, oil, particulates, and microbial content as applicable)?
Are point-of-use filters on gases and process air integrity-tested or qualified, and are gas systems included in the routine utility monitoring and maintenance program?

Is there a documented environmental monitoring program for classified areas covering viable air, surface, personnel, and non-viable particle monitoring, with locations selected by risk and rationale documented?

Are EM sample locations, frequencies, methods, incubation conditions, and limits defined in approved procedures, and is the program based on a documented risk assessment rather than arbitrary point selection?

Are environmental monitoring results trended individually and collectively, with adverse trends and excursions investigated, including assessment of impact on product and identification of recovered organisms to the level required?

Is microbial identification performed on isolates from critical (Grade A/B) areas and excursions, and are these isolates compared against the facility flora and used to challenge disinfectant effectiveness?

Is the cleaning and disinfection program for facilities documented with defined agents, contact times, rotation including a sporicidal agent, and is disinfectant efficacy validated against representative facility isolates?
Are cleaning agents and disinfectants prepared, stored, labeled with expiry, and used within their validated in-use shelf life, with records of preparation and application retained?
Is there a documented integrated pest management program with defined scope and a qualified or contracted provider, including a current trending log of pest-control device activity?
Are pest control chemicals (pesticides, rodenticides) restricted to approved, GMP-reviewed agents and applied only in a manner that cannot contaminate product or product-contact surfaces?

Is the building exterior and shell (roof, doors, dock seals, windows, drains with air breaks) maintained to prevent ingress of pests, water, and contamination, and verified through routine facility walkdowns?

Is there a preventive maintenance program for facilities and utilities (HVAC, water, gases, drains, building systems) with defined frequencies, work orders, and records showing PM is performed on schedule?

Are maintenance and lubricants/materials that could contact product surfaces controlled (food-grade or otherwise qualified), and is post-maintenance cleaning, verification, or requalification performed before the area or system returns to GMP use?

Are changes to facilities and utilities (room reclassification, HVAC modification, water loop changes, equipment relocation) managed through change control with impact assessment, requalification, and update of drawings and procedures?

Are instruments used to monitor and control facilities and utilities (pressure gauges, particle counters, temperature/humidity probes, conductivity/TOC meters, flow meters) included in a calibration program with current, traceable calibration status?

Are utility and environmental monitoring systems that store GMP data (building management systems, EM data systems) validated, access-controlled, with audit trails reviewed and data backed up per data integrity expectations?

Is there a defined response to utility failures and excursions (power loss, HVAC down, water out of spec, pressure loss) with procedures covering product impact assessment, area recovery, and requalification before resuming operations?

Are drains in classified and process areas designed with air breaks, trapped, and located and maintained to prevent backflow and contamination, and are floor drains absent from Grade A/B aseptic processing areas?

Is housekeeping in non-classified GMP areas (warehouse, corridors, mechanical spaces) controlled by procedure with records, so that dust, clutter, and uncontrolled storage do not create contamination or pest harborage?

Are temperature-controlled storage areas (cold rooms, freezers, ambient warehouses) mapped, monitored with alarms, and qualified to show uniform conditions, with excursion handling tied to product disposition?

For cell and gene therapy or live-virus operations, are containment level and biosafety controls (BSL classification, negative pressure where needed, segregation of replication-competent material) defined, qualified, and reconciled with the cleanliness cascade?

sections.name: Sterile / Aseptic Processing & Contamination Control
sections.intro: An inspector in this area is testing whether your contamination control strategy is a living, data-driven document that connects cleanroom design, environmental monitoring, personnel behavior, aseptic process simulations, sterilizing filtration, and container closure integrity into one defensible system. Expect close scrutiny of EU GMP Annex 1 (2022) expectations, FDA's aseptic processing guidance, classification and qualification data, monitoring trends and excursions, media fill design and failures, smoke study evidence, and the science behind each acceptance criterion you have set.

questions.q: Do you maintain a single documented Contamination Control Strategy (CCS) that covers the whole site or facility, references all relevant processes and controls, and is reviewed and updated on a defined cycle as required by Annex 1?

Does the CCS describe the technical and organizational controls as a connected system (facility design, utilities, personnel, materials flow, EM, process, and product) rather than listing controls in isolation, and does it identify residual risks?
Can you show that the CCS is informed by risk assessments (for example QRM per ICH Q9) and that the effectiveness of each control is monitored, trended, and fed back into the strategy?

Are your cleanrooms and clean air devices classified to the correct ISO 14644-1 / Annex 1 grades (A, B, C, D) for the operations performed, with particle limits defined for both at-rest and in-operation states for 0.5 and 5.0 micron particles?

Is the Grade A zone (critical processing) maintained with unidirectional airflow at the velocity range stated in the current guidance, measured at the working position, and supported by airflow visualization evidence?

Do you requalify cleanrooms at the defined frequency (for example Grade A and B at least every 6 months, Grade C and D at least every 12 months) and after significant changes, covering classification, recovery, airflow, and pressure differentials?
Are room pressure differentials (typically 10 to 15 Pa between adjacent grades) continuously monitored, alarmed, and recorded, with defined actions when differentials are lost?

Do you have documented air change rates, HEPA filter integrity test results (for example DOP/PAO), and recovery test data demonstrating each graded area returns to its classified state within the qualified time?

Is your environmental monitoring program based on a risk-based assessment that defines locations, frequencies, methods, and limits derived from contamination risk and supported by qualification data, not just copied from a template?

Does Grade A receive continuous viable and total particle monitoring during the full duration of critical processing, with the monitoring system capable of detecting and alarming excursions in real time?
Have you set EM alert and action limits that are scientifically justified, with the Grade A expectation that viable counts should be effectively zero (no growth) and any recovery investigated?

Do you monitor viable air (active and passive/settle plates), surfaces (contact plates and swabs), and personnel (gloves and gown) at appropriate frequencies for each grade, including operator monitoring at the end of critical interventions?
Are EM data trended over time (by location, organism, grade, and shift), with adverse trends formally identified and investigated even when individual results are within limits?

Do you identify recovered microorganisms to an appropriate taxonomic level (typically genus and species where indicated), and use that identification to assess contamination routes and update the CCS?
Do you maintain a microbial identification library and use it to detect objectionable organisms, recurring isolates, and possible disinfectant-resistant flora?
Are EM excursions and out-of-limit results investigated with root cause analysis, impact assessment on product released or in process, and CAPA, with batch disposition decisions documented?
Is your cleaning and disinfection program qualified to demonstrate efficacy against the site's flora, including use of a sporicidal agent on a defined frequency and rotation justified by data?
Are disinfectants prepared, sterilized where required for Grade A/B, used within validated in-use shelf life, and applied with documented contact times?

Do gowning qualification and requalification programs exist for all personnel entering Grade A/B areas, including initial qualification, periodic requalification, and a defined number of successful gowning assessments?
Are personnel entering aseptic areas trained and assessed on aseptic technique and behavior, with their gowning competency tied to participation in media fills?
Is the number of personnel in the cleanroom limited and controlled, with movements minimized, and are interventions (inherent and corrective) defined, listed, and risk-assessed?

Do aseptic process simulations (media fills) cover each filling line, container/closure configuration, and shift, with initial line qualification of three consecutive successful runs per line/process and periodic revalidation at least twice a year per line/shift thereafter?

Are media fills designed to represent worst-case conditions, including maximum run duration, maximum number of operators and interventions, line speed extremes, and the most challenging container sizes?
Does the media fill incorporate all routine and reasonably expected non-routine interventions, with each intervention recorded and the units associated with interventions tracked and incubated?

Is the media fill target zero contaminated units with any contaminated unit triggering investigation, and are run-size-based action limits applied per FDA aseptic guidance, consistent with the Annex 1 expectation of no contamination?
Are filled media units incubated at the defined temperatures and durations, inspected by qualified personnel, and is every contaminated unit investigated for organism identity and probable cause?

Does a failed media fill trigger a full investigation, assessment of all product manufactured since the last successful simulation, and revalidation before resuming aseptic production where warranted?
For terminally sterilized products, do you justify and document the choice of aseptic processing versus terminal sterilization, and apply terminal sterilization where the product can withstand it?

Are sterilizing-grade filters validated for the specific product, including bacterial retention (challenge with Brevundimonas diminuta at the appropriate level), compatibility, extractables/leachables, and adsorption effects?

Are sterilizing filters integrity tested (for example bubble point, diffusive flow, or pressure hold) both before use where required and after use, with results recorded and linked to batch disposition?
Where redundant (two) sterilizing filters are used, is the post-use integrity-test failure handling defined, including which filter is considered the sterilizing filter?
Are maximum filtration time, pressure, and filter usage (single use versus reuse) defined, validated, and controlled, with bioburden limits set and monitored on the solution before filtration?

Do you perform airflow visualization (smoke) studies under dynamic in-operation conditions for Grade A zones, demonstrating unidirectional flow and absence of turbulence at the critical points, including during representative interventions?

Are smoke studies documented with recorded video, reviewed and approved, repeated after relevant changes to equipment or layout, and used as evidence supporting the aseptic line design and intervention list?

Is container closure integrity (CCI) assured through a validated CCIT method (for example deterministic methods such as helium leak or vacuum decay where appropriate) rather than relying solely on probabilistic dye ingress?

Is CCI verified across the product lifecycle, including container/closure qualification, validation at the relevant conditions (for example after lyophilization or cold storage), and ongoing monitoring or stability checks?
Where Restricted Access Barrier Systems (RABS) or isolators are used, are they qualified, with glove integrity testing, bio-decontamination cycle validation, and leak testing performed and trended?

For isolators, is the bio-decontamination cycle (for example vaporized hydrogen peroxide) validated with biological indicators and is residual agent shown not to affect product or media fill outcomes?

Are pre-use post-sterilization integrity tests (PUPSIT) of sterilizing filters performed, or is a documented risk-based justification in place where PUPSIT is not applied, consistent with Annex 1 expectations?
Is water and steam used in sterile manufacturing (WFI, clean steam) qualified, continuously or routinely monitored for endotoxin and bioburden, and controlled within the CCS?
Are single-use systems and components used in aseptic processing qualified for sterility, integrity, and extractables/leachables, with supplier controls and incoming verification defined?
Is the time from compounding through filtration to filling controlled within validated hold times, and is in-process bioburden and endotoxin held within defined limits at each stage?

sections.name: Microbiology Laboratory Controls
sections.intro: An inspector in this area is testing whether your microbiology lab generates reliable, defensible data: that media works before it is used, that environmental monitoring and sterility, endotoxin, and bioburden testing follow validated methods, that organisms are identified to the level needed for investigations, and that micro records get a real second-person review with full data integrity. Expect close scrutiny of growth promotion records, raw EM and incubation data, out-of-specification and out-of-limit investigations, and the audit trails behind any computerized micro instruments.

questions.q: Is every lot of prepared and purchased microbiological media subjected to growth promotion testing using the compendial challenge organisms before the media is released for use, with results documented and reviewed?

Are growth promotion acceptance criteria defined per the relevant USP chapter (for example recovery within a factor of 2 of the calibrated inoculum for quantitative media, and clear growth for qualitative media), rather than left to analyst judgment?
Is media that fails growth promotion or is past its expiry physically quarantined or destroyed so it cannot reach the bench?

Are the challenge organism stocks used for growth promotion and method suitability traceable to a recognized culture collection (for example ATCC), passage-controlled to no more than five passages from the reference, and stored and maintained under documented conditions?

Is your environmental monitoring program based on a documented, risk-based rationale that defines sample sites, frequencies, methods, and grade-appropriate alert and action limits tied to a current facility and cleanroom classification map?

Do EM action limits for the most critical Grade A or ISO 5 zones reflect that any recovery is significant, and are limits for viable and total particulate consistent with Annex 1 and USP/ISO expectations for the room grade?

Is EM raw data (plate counts, active air sample volumes, settle plate exposure times, contact plate sites, incubation start and end times) recorded contemporaneously and attributable to the analyst who performed it?
Are EM incubation conditions (temperature ranges, sequence, and total duration across both incubation temperatures) defined and justified?

Can you demonstrate that EM excursions (alert and action) trigger a documented investigation with trend evaluation, microbial identification of recovered isolates, and impact assessment on product made during the affected period?
Is EM data trended over time (per site, per room, per shift) so that adverse trends and recurring isolates are detected and acted on before they become action-limit failures?

Are personnel monitoring (gown and glove sampling) results captured, trended, and linked to a qualification and requalification program with defined action on repeated excursions, including operator retraining or exclusion from aseptic areas?
Do you maintain a current EM isolate library and a house flora characterization that informs identification, disinfectant efficacy studies, and media fill challenge organism selection?

Is the sterility test performed by membrane filtration as the default method per USP <71>, with direct inoculation used only where filtration is not feasible and that choice justified for the product?

Has method suitability (bacteriostasis and fungistasis) testing been performed for each product to demonstrate that the test conditions allow recovery of low numbers of challenge organisms in the presence of the product?
Are sterility tests conducted in a qualified isolator or Grade A environment with documented environmental monitoring of each test session?
Do you correctly apply the USP <71> sample quantities and minimum number of articles tested based on batch size and container volume, rather than testing fewer units than the compendia require?
Is the full 14-day sterility incubation observed and documented, with negative and positive (growth) controls run each session and any control failure invalidating the test?

When a sterility test shows growth, do you conduct a thorough investigation per USP <71> criteria before any retest, with the original result invalidated only on documented evidence of a laboratory cause, never simply because a retest passed?

Is bacterial endotoxin testing (BET) per USP <85> validated for each product, including a documented inhibition/enhancement (interference) study and a justified maximum valid dilution (MVD) calculation tied to the endotoxin limit?
Is the endotoxin limit for each product derived from the correct threshold (K/M) using the route of administration and maximum human dose, and documented rather than carried over generically?

Are LAL reagent, control standard endotoxin (CSE), and water for BET controlled with documented standard curves or gel-clot label claim confirmation, valid positive product controls, and lot-specific reagent sensitivity verification?

If a recombinant Factor C or other non-LAL endotoxin method is used, has it been validated as an alternative microbiological method per USP <1223> (with recombinant Factor C addressed in USP <1085.1>/<85>) including a documented equivalence comparison to the compendial method?

Is in-process and pre-sterilization bioburden testing performed at defined hold and process points with established alert and action limits that feed the sterilization and sterile filtration validation assumptions?

Has the bioburden recovery method been validated for each matrix (suitability/recovery study) so that the count reported reflects the true bioburden and is not suppressed by the product or recovery technique?

Are bioburden results, including any objectionable or sterilization-resistant organisms recovered before terminal sterilization or filtration, identified and assessed against the validated bioburden assumptions for that process?

Is microbial identification performed to the level needed for the decision at hand (genus/species for investigations, sterility positives, EM action excursions, and objectionable organism assessment) rather than stopping at gram stain and colony morphology?

Is the identification system (for example MALDI-TOF or genotypic/16S sequencing) qualified, with current databases, verified performance, and a defined approach when an isolate cannot be confidently matched to the library?

Do you have a documented definition of objectionable organisms for your products and routes of administration, and is recovery of such an organism in non-sterile product treated as a quality event with patient-risk assessment?

For non-sterile products, are microbial enumeration and tests for specified microorganisms performed and validated per USP <61> and <62>, with limits per USP <1111> appropriate to the dosage form and route?

Are all micro test records subjected to a documented second-person (peer) review that checks raw data, calculations, plate counts, incubation times, controls, and conformance to the method before results are reported or released?
Does the data review include verification that growth and other controls met acceptance criteria for the run, so that an invalid control set blocks reporting of associated sample results?

Are micro out-of-specification (OOS) and out-of-limit (EM/bioburden) investigations governed by a procedure that distinguishes assignable laboratory error from a true result, prohibits invalidating data without documented cause, and avoids testing into compliance?
Are plate counts, including TNTC handling, dilution selection, and rounding rules, defined in procedures so that count interpretation is consistent across analysts and defensible on review?

Do computerized micro instruments and systems (plate readers, MALDI, endotoxin readers, incubator monitoring, LIMS) have audit trails that are enabled, reviewed as part of record review, and protected from analyst alteration or deletion?

Are micro instruments (incubators, autoclaves for media/waste, biosafety cabinets, isolators, balances, particle counters, endotoxin readers) on a documented calibration, qualification, and preventive maintenance schedule with current status verifiable at use?

Is analyst qualification for each micro technique (aseptic technique, plate reading, sterility test, endotoxin, identification) documented, with periodic requalification and ongoing competency assessment such as aseptic technique monitoring?
Are reference and working culture stocks, media, and reagents traceable through a controlled inventory so that the specific lot used in any test can be reconstructed during an investigation?

Is the disinfectant and sporicide program supported by a documented disinfectant efficacy study against house flora and reference organisms on actual facility surface materials, with defined rotation and contact times?
Are micro sample chain-of-custody, hold times, and storage conditions from collection to test controlled and documented so that delays or improper storage cannot silently bias results?

Is there an overall periodic review or trend report for the micro lab (EM, water, bioburden, sterility positives, isolate trends, GP failures) that feeds the quality system and management review with documented conclusions and actions?

sections.name: Materials Management & Supplier Qualification
sections.intro: An inspector in this area is testing whether you actually control what comes into your process: how components, raw materials, and packaging are tested or released on the basis of a supplier certificate, how suppliers are qualified and kept qualified, and how materials are sampled, identified, segregated, stored, and used within expiry or retest dates. Expect questions that trace a specific lot from receipt through release, identity testing, status labeling, and use in a batch, plus the supporting supplier file, quality agreement, and CoA reliance justification.

questions.q: Do you have an approved procedure that defines receipt, identification, sampling, testing, and release/rejection for each category of incoming material (active ingredients, excipients, primary packaging, labeling, process aids), with clear roles and the decision authority for accept versus reject?

Is at least one specific identity test performed on each lot of each incoming component as required by 21 CFR 211.84(d)(1), rather than relying on the supplier's identity statement alone, and are drug product containers and closures tested or examined for conformance (with CoA reliance where justified) per 211.84(d)(3)?

When you accept a supplier's certificate of analysis in lieu of full testing for an attribute, have you established the reliability of that supplier's test results through your own periodic full analysis as required by 211.84(d)(2)?

Does each certificate of analysis you rely on contain the actual numerical test results against the specification (not just pass/fail), the test methods used, the lot or batch number that matches the received material, and an authorized signature or equivalent control?

Do you confirm that the specifications and test methods cited on a supplier CoA match your approved internal specifications, and do you have a defined action when a CoA shows a method or limit different from yours?

Are representative samples collected for each incoming lot using a documented, statistically or scientifically justified sampling plan (for example number of containers sampled and amount per container), with the rationale tied to the number of containers received and the variability of the material per 211.84(b) and (c)?

Are samples taken under conditions that protect against contamination and degradation, with sampling tools and the opened containers handled and resealed to prevent cross-contamination, and are sampled containers identified as having been opened?
Are containers visually examined on receipt for damage, correct labeling, broken seals, and contamination, and is that examination recorded before the material is sampled or accepted per 211.82?

Does every incoming material carry a clear status label (quarantine, under test/sampled, approved/released, or rejected) at all times, so that status is unambiguous to anyone in the warehouse without consulting a separate system?

Are quarantined, under-test, approved, and rejected materials physically separated, or controlled by a validated electronic inventory system that prevents the issue or use of any material not yet released?

Is there a control that physically or electronically prevents released material from being dispensed or used while it is in quarantine or after it has been rejected, and has that control been challenged or tested?
Are rejected materials clearly identified and controlled to prevent their use, and is there a defined disposition path (return to supplier, destruction) with records of the final disposition?

Does each lot of incoming material receive a unique receipt or control number that lets you trace it from receipt through sampling, testing, release, dispensing, and into the specific batches that used it?

Are approved materials retested or reexamined after a defined storage period or when conditions suggest they may have deteriorated, and is material that has exceeded its retest or expiry date moved to quarantine and prevented from use per 211.87?
Is a documented basis (stability or supplier data) maintained for any assigned retest interval, and is material fully retested or used before that interval lapses?

Are incoming materials stored under the conditions specified by the manufacturer or your specification (temperature, humidity, light, cold chain), with monitoring records and excursion handling for materials that require controlled storage?
Is stock issued on a first-expiry-first-out (or first-in-first-out) basis, with a control that flags or blocks any attempt to bypass the oldest or nearest-expiry material?

Do you maintain an approved supplier list (ASL) tied to the specific material and supplier site, and is purchasing prevented or flagged when material is ordered from a supplier or site not on that list?

Is there a documented, risk-based supplier qualification program that classifies suppliers by the criticality of the material they provide and defines the qualification activities (questionnaire, document review, on-site or remote audit, sample testing) required for each tier?

For critical material suppliers (for example active ingredient, primary container/closure, sterile-product components), have you performed and documented an initial qualification audit or a justified alternative, and is requalification scheduled on a defined periodic interval?
Do supplier audits have a written report with findings, a CAPA response from the supplier, and tracking of those CAPAs to closure, and does the qualification status reflect the outcome of the audit?

Is there a current quality agreement in place with each contract manufacturer, contract testing lab, and critical material supplier that defines GMP responsibilities, change notification, deviation/OOS handling, and audit rights, signed by both quality units?

Does the quality agreement require the supplier to notify you in advance of changes to the material, its manufacturing process, site, or specifications, and do you have evidence that change notifications are actually received and assessed for impact?

Is the qualification status of each supplier monitored on an ongoing basis using performance data (rejected lots, complaints, OOS rate, on-time delivery, deviations), with criteria for disqualification or for-cause audits?

When a material attribute or supplier changes, is there a defined process to assess the impact on your registered product, on validation, and on whether the supplier remains qualified, before the changed material is used in production?
Are reduced testing or skip-lot testing schemes (where used) supported by a documented history of supplier performance and a procedure that defines when full testing must resume?
Do you maintain reserve/retention samples of incoming active ingredients and components where required, in the proper quantity and storage conditions, for the defined retention period?

Is there a defined supply chain mapping and traceability for each material back to the original manufacturer (not just the distributor or broker), so you know the actual source of the material you receive?

For active pharmaceutical ingredients, do you assess and control the risk of nitrosamine impurities and other process-related impurities at the supplier, with supplier data or your own testing supporting the conclusion?
For excipients, have you performed a documented risk assessment of GMP requirements appropriate to their use and source, and qualified excipient suppliers commensurate with that risk?

For materials used in sterile or aseptic manufacturing, do you control and where appropriate test for bioburden and endotoxin on incoming components, and are sterile single-use or pre-sterilized components qualified and their sterility documentation reviewed?

For biological starting materials, cell banks, sera, media, and other biological raw materials, is the supplier qualified for adventitious agent and TSE/BSE risk control, with the supporting certificates and country-of-origin documentation on file?

For cell and gene therapy starting materials (for example viral vectors, plasmids, donor-derived cells, ancillary/raw materials of biological origin), are suppliers qualified to the standard appropriate for ATMP/CGT use, including donor eligibility and chain-of-identity/chain-of-custody controls for patient-specific materials?

For combination products, are device constituent parts and their suppliers controlled under the applicable purchasing controls, with acceptance activities and supplier qualification appropriate to the device constituent?

For printed packaging and labeling components, are these treated as critical materials with controlled receipt, reconciliation, online or independent verification, and segregated storage to prevent label mix-ups per 211.122 and 211.125?
When incoming material fails a specification or shows an out-of-specification CoA result, is it handled through your deviation/OOS process with a documented disposition decision?

Are the inventory, sampling, and release decisions in your computerized materials management or ERP system supported by access controls, audit trails, and validation, so that status changes (quarantine to release) are attributable and cannot be made by unauthorized users?

Is there a documented process for handling returned, reworked, or salvaged materials and for dedicated/campaigned materials, ensuring their identity and status are controlled and they are not reintroduced without quality approval?

Can you trace, for a randomly chosen finished batch, every incoming material lot used, and produce the receipt record, CoA, identity test result, release record, and status history for each within the inspection timeframe?

sections.name: Production & In-Process Controls / Batch Records
