sections.intro: An investigator will look for master and executed batch records that match approved processes, controlled in-process testing tied to real acceptance criteria, documented line clearance, honest yield reconciliation, and contemporaneous record completion, with a meaningful independent batch record review before any lot is released. The questions below test whether your production records and controls would hold up under that scrutiny.

questions.q: Is there an approved master production and control record for each product and batch size, with the active version controlled so that only the current approved version can be issued for use (211.186)?

Does each master batch record list the product name and strength, complete identity of all components, accurate quantities or theoretical batch size, and a statement of theoretical yield with the expected percentage ranges?

Does the master record include complete manufacturing and control instructions, sampling and testing procedures, in-process specifications, and the special notations and precautions a step requires (211.186)?
Are master records, and any change to them, dated, signed, and independently checked by a second person, with the change controlled through your change management system?
Is each executed batch record a true, accurate reproduction of the appropriate master record, with the issued copy verified for completeness and correctness before it goes to the floor (211.188)?

Does each executed batch record document the actual performance of every significant step, including dates, times where required, identity of major equipment and lines used, and identity of the persons performing and checking each step (211.188)?
Are component, container, and closure lot numbers and quantities actually used recorded in the executed record so each batch can be traced to the specific materials consumed (211.188)?
Are entries made at the time the activity is performed (contemporaneously) by the person performing it, rather than recorded later from memory or transcribed from scrap notes?

Do record entries follow good documentation practice, with permanent ink, no obliteration of original entries, single-line cross-outs that keep the original readable, and corrections initialed, dated, and given a reason where appropriate?
Is there a defined rule for how blanks, N/A entries, and unused fields are handled so that no field is left blank in a way that could be completed later (211.188)?
Where the batch record is electronic (MES or eBR), is the system validated for its intended use with entries attributable through unique individual logins (Part 11)?
Are in-process controls and tests defined for each step where attributes affect product quality, with acceptance criteria that match the validated process and the approved specification (211.110)?

Are in-process samples taken at the defined frequency and locations, by qualified people using approved methods, with results recorded and judged against the acceptance criteria before the batch moves forward?

When an in-process result is out of the established limit, is the batch held, the event investigated, and the disposition documented before further processing rather than the step simply being repeated until it passes?
Are critical process parameters and critical quality attributes from your process validation reflected in the batch record limits, so the record actually enforces the validated state of control?

Is a documented line clearance performed and verified by a second person before starting a new product, strength, or batch, confirming removal of prior product, components, labels, and records from the area and equipment (211.130)?
Does line clearance cover the equipment, the line, the immediate area, and the documentation, and is it recorded with the names of the person performing and the person verifying it?
Are actual yields and percentages of theoretical yield determined and recorded at the conclusion of each appropriate phase of manufacturing, processing, packaging, and holding (211.103)?
Are percentages of theoretical yield reconciled against predetermined limits, and is any yield outside those limits investigated and explained before the batch is released (211.192)?

For packaging and labeling operations, is reconciliation of issued, used, and returned or destroyed labels performed, with discrepancies investigated, since unexplained label shortages can signal mislabeling (211.125, 211.134)?
Is there a defined, signed-off review of each batch record by a qualified person before the batch is dispositioned, and is that review separate from the people who performed the work?

Does the batch record review by the quality unit confirm that production and control records are complete, that all required steps and checks were performed and signed, and that the batch meets all established specifications before release (211.192)?

Does the review explicitly check that there are no unexplained discrepancies and that the batch and its components conform to specifications, with any failure of a batch or component to meet specs thoroughly investigated whether or not the batch was distributed (211.192)?
Are deviations, planned and unplanned, recorded against the affected batch, investigated for product impact, and closed or assessed for impact before the batch is released?
Is the final authority to approve or reject each batch held by the quality unit, and is that release decision documented with a signature and date (211.22)?

Are reprocessing or reworking activities of a batch performed only against an approved procedure, justified, documented in the batch record, and approved by the quality unit, rather than handled informally on the floor?
Are the operators, line clearance verifiers, and reviewers trained and qualified for the specific tasks they perform and sign for, with that training current and documented?

Are weighing and dispensing of components performed against the batch record with the actual weights recorded, equipment identity captured, and a second-person verification of the dispensed materials (211.101)?

Is the equipment used in each batch confirmed to be within its calibration and qualification status and to have a current cleaning status at the time of use, with that confirmation tied to the batch record?
Are process times, hold times, and bioburden or microbial control limits between steps defined and recorded so time-sensitive intermediates are not held beyond validated limits?

For aseptic and sterile operations, does the batch record capture line clearance, environmental monitoring results, fill weights or volumes, interventions, and the start and stop times needed to assess each lot against the validated aseptic process (211.110, 211.113)?

For cell and gene therapy or other patient-specific or short-shelf-life products, does the batch record support chain of identity and chain of custody and allow disposition within the compressed timelines without shortcutting the required review?

For combination products, do the production records and in-process controls cover both the drug constituent and the device constituent, including the device assembly, joining, and any associated in-process device checks under the streamlined Part 4 approach?
Are repeated deviations, recurring yield shortfalls, or recurring in-process failures trended across batches and fed into CAPA, rather than each event being closed in isolation?
When execution departs from the written instruction, is the actual as-run condition captured and routed through the deviation system so the executed record reflects what actually happened (211.100)?

Are batch records and supporting in-process data retained for the required period and readily retrievable for inspection, with consistency between the batch record, equipment logs, and any electronic system data?

sections.name: Packaging & Labeling Controls
sections.intro: An investigator focuses here because labeling and packaging mix-ups are a leading cause of recalls and a recurring 483 theme. Expect close scrutiny of label issuance and reconciliation, line clearance between products and batches, coding and printed-material verification, expiry and lot application, serialization/DSCSA data handling, and the physical and procedural defenses against product, label, and component mix-ups, all traceable through the batch packaging record.

questions.q: Are all labels and other labeling, including inserts and cartons, stored and held in a secured area with access restricted to authorized personnel so that issuance can be controlled?
Is labeling issued only by the labeling control unit against the specific approved batch record?
Are label reconciliation discrepancies that exceed the defined acceptable variance limit investigated and resolved before the batch is released?
Are excess, obsolete, or returned labels and printed packaging components destroyed or rendered unusable in a documented, witnessed manner rather than returned to general stock?
When labeling is gang-printed or cut, or when similar labels are used for different products or strengths, are special control procedures in place to prevent label mix-ups?
Is each lot of printed packaging material examined and approved against an approved specimen or master before it is released for use, and is the examination recorded?
Are incoming printed packaging components held under quarantine, with each lot uniquely identified, until released to approved status?

Is a documented line clearance performed and recorded by a second responsible person before each packaging run begins, verifying that no materials, products, or documents from a prior operation remain on the line?

Does the line clearance check specifically confirm removal of prior product, prior labels and printed components, and prior batch documentation from the line and surrounding area, including waste and reject bins?
Are physical or spatial separations, or other defined controls, used to prevent two different products or batches from being packaged on adjacent lines at the same time when mix-up risk exists?
Is the printing and coding of lot number and expiration date verified at startup and at defined intervals during the run, with the verification recorded in the batch packaging record?
Where online printing devices (such as ink-jet or laser coders) are used, is the imprint verified against the batch record specification at defined points and the verification recorded?
Is the expiration date applied to each unit calculated from the correct date basis, such as date of manufacture, and verified to match the approved shelf life in the batch record?
Are vision/electronic verification systems for labels and codes qualified and challenged with known defective samples to confirm they reliably reject defects?
Are defined actions (such as quarantine of suspect units and rework) specified and followed when automated code/label verification equipment fails a check?
Are representative finished, fully coded and labeled samples collected and retained from the packaging run for inspection and record?
Is the actual yield and percentage of theoretical yield for the packaging operation calculated, recorded, and investigated when it falls outside established limits?
Does the batch packaging record document the identity and quantity of each labeling and packaging component used, the operators and checkers involved, and the equipment and line used?
Are tamper-evident features applied and verified as required for the product type during packaging?
Is each finished package inspected to ensure it contains the correct labeling, including the correct insert and any required carton, before release?
Are products that are similar in appearance, name, or packaging configuration identified as look-alike or sound-alike risks, with specific controls applied to prevent mix-ups?
Are all rejected, defective, or unlabeled units, and any units with illegible or incorrect codes, segregated, controlled, and reconciled so they cannot reach finished stock?
Are set-down or partial batches (such as bulk held between filling and packaging) handled under a defined procedure that includes hold-time limits?
Are changes to packaging or labeling components, artwork, or coding controlled through change control, with regulatory and label-content impact assessed before implementation?
Is artwork and label content version-controlled, with the approved current version uniquely identified and the only version available to printing and the line?

Does the product serial number, lot number, expiration date, and product identifier applied to saleable units conform to DSCSA requirements for product identifier and the standardized numerical identifier?
Is the product identifier encoded in a 2D data matrix barcode on each package and homogeneous case as required under DSCSA?
Are serialization and aggregation data generated, captured, and stored so that the parent-child relationship of units to cases and pallets is accurate and verifiable?
Is transaction information and transaction statement data, including the serialized product identifier, exchanged electronically and retained for the period required under DSCSA?
Is there a defined process to detect, investigate, and respond to suspect or illegitimate product, including notification and quarantine, as required under DSCSA?

Is the serialization system, including the level 1 through level 4 software and data exchange, validated and under change control, with serial number range management preventing duplicate or unauthorized numbers?
Are sampling and reconciliation of serial numbers performed so that commissioned, decommissioned, rejected, and shipped serials are fully accounted for at the end of each run?
Are operators and checkers trained and qualified for the specific packaging line, coding, and verification tasks they perform, with training documented and current?
Is packaging and labeling equipment cleaned, set up, and verified between products and batches per procedure, with cleaning and changeover recorded?
For combination products, is the device constituent labeling, including required device markings and any unique device identification, controlled and applied correctly alongside the drug labeling?
For products requiring special storage, such as cold-chain or controlled room temperature, is the required storage statement present on the label and consistent with the approved conditions?

Are deviations occurring during packaging, such as coder failures, jams causing potential mix-up, or reconciliation failures, documented and investigated with impact on the batch assessed before disposition?
Does QA review the completed batch packaging record, including line clearance, reconciliation, coding verification, and serialization data, before releasing the packaged batch?

sections.name: Training & Competency
sections.intro: An investigator or auditor in this area verifies that everyone who performs GxP work is qualified by education, training, and experience for the specific tasks they do, that training happens before the work (not after), and that records prove it. Expect them to trace a named employee from a job role to a training curriculum to documented training events to demonstrated on-the-job competency, and to probe how retraining is triggered after errors, deviations, and procedure changes.

questions.q: Is there a current, approved procedure that defines how training is planned, delivered, assessed, documented, and tracked, with clear roles for the trainee, trainer, supervisor, and Quality?

Does each GxP role have a defined, documented training curriculum (training matrix) that lists the specific SOPs, regulations, and skills required for that role, rather than a generic one-size-fits-all list?
Can you show that every person performing a GxP activity is qualified for that specific task by a combination of education, training, and experience, as required by 21 CFR 211.25?

Is initial GMP/GxP orientation training completed and documented before a new or transferred employee performs any independent GxP work, with a clear record of what they were and were not yet authorized to do during onboarding?

Is task-specific procedure training documented as completed before the employee independently performs that task, so that no training record shows a person performing work earlier than the date they were trained on it?

When you trace a random recent batch record or test result back to the people who performed and reviewed it, can you produce a training record showing each of them was trained on the applicable procedures on or before that date?

Does the training program distinguish between read-and-understand acknowledgement, instructor-led training, and hands-on qualification, and is the method matched to the risk and complexity of the task?

For tasks where reading alone is not enough, is competency assessed through a documented method such as a written quiz, a practical demonstration, or supervised performance, rather than just a signature confirming the SOP was read?

Are on-the-job training and competency qualifications documented with the trainer's identity, the trainee's identity, the date, the specific task qualified, and an objective acceptance criterion that was met?
Are trainers and assessors themselves qualified and authorized to train and assess for the tasks they sign off, with that trainer qualification documented?
Is there a defined frequency for refresher GMP/GxP and data integrity training, and can you show it is being delivered on schedule with records to prove completion?

Does the program define and apply documented retraining triggers, for example deviations, repeat human errors, failed self-inspections or audit findings, complaints, and recurring documentation errors traced to a specific person or step?

When a deviation or CAPA root cause is identified as inadequate training or human error, can you show the corresponding retraining was actually completed and verified as effective, rather than closed as a paper action only?
When a procedure is revised, does a controlled process ensure affected staff are retrained before the revised version takes effect?
Are training records (paper or electronic) attributable, legible, contemporaneous, original, and accurate (ALCOA), with controls that prevent backdating?
Are individual training records readily retrievable on request, so that a complete, current training history for any named employee can be produced within minutes during an inspection?
Is there a defined process to identify training gaps (people not trained on procedures they need) and to prevent or detect work being performed by anyone with an open required-training gap?
Are temporary, contract, agency, and visiting staff who perform GxP tasks trained and qualified to the same standard as employees, with their training documented and controlled by your system?

Is data integrity awareness training (ALCOA+ principles, good documentation practices, no shared logins, no backdating, error correction rules) delivered to all relevant GxP staff and refreshed periodically?
Are good documentation practices (how to make entries, correct errors, handle blank fields, and sign and date) explicitly trained and assessed, not just assumed?
Is the effectiveness of the overall training program evaluated periodically, for example by reviewing whether human error events and repeat deviations are trending down after training interventions?

If training is managed in an electronic learning management or training system, is that system validated, access-controlled, and audit-trailed, with controls that prevent users from altering their own training completion records?

If completion records are signed electronically, do the electronic signatures meet 21 CFR Part 11 (or Annex 11) requirements for unique attribution, signature meaning, and the link between signature and record?

Is there a documented authorization or qualification list that ties named individuals to the specific operations they are permitted to perform (for example release, batch record review, sampling, aseptic intervention), kept current as people qualify or lose qualification?
When someone has not performed a qualified task for an extended period, is there a defined re-qualification or competency-refresh requirement before they resume that task independently?

Does management training cover GxP responsibilities, escalation duties, and the expectation to support a quality culture, so supervisors who approve training and authorize work are themselves prepared for that role?

For personnel working in or supporting aseptic processing and clean areas, is there documented gowning qualification and aseptic technique qualification, with periodic re-qualification and a defined link to failed gown-monitoring or media-fill results?

Are operators who perform or supervise aseptic process simulations (media fills) trained and qualified, and is participation in media fills used as part of their ongoing aseptic qualification, consistent with Annex 1 expectations?

For cell and gene therapy operations, are staff trained and qualified on aseptic handling, segregation and changeover to prevent cross-contamination and mix-ups between patient-specific or donor-specific materials, and on chain-of-identity and chain-of-custody procedures?

For biologics manufacturing, are personnel trained and qualified on contamination control, viral safety handling, and the specific containment and segregation practices relevant to live biological materials they work with?
For API manufacturing, are operators trained and qualified to the GMP expectations of ICH Q7 for the specific unit operations and the stage of manufacture they support?

For combination products, are staff trained on the additional constituent-part GMP controls that apply under 21 CFR Part 4 (for example the specified device or drug requirements beyond the base GMP determined by the primary mode of action), matched to the tasks they perform?
Are personnel who perform packaging and labeling operations specifically trained and qualified on line clearance, label reconciliation, and the controls that prevent mix-ups and labeling errors?

For clinical and GCP-supporting roles, are study staff trained on the protocol, current ICH E6 GCP, and their delegated tasks, with that training documented before they perform the task and reflected on the delegation log?
Is training on health, hygiene, and clothing requirements provided and documented for personnel entering production and control areas, as required by GMP personnel provisions?

When training is outsourced to a vendor or external trainer (for example equipment or software supplier training), is that training captured in your records and assessed for sufficiency rather than just accepted on trust?
Can you demonstrate that supervisors do not sign off their own required training, and that segregation exists so that training completion is independently verified where the procedure requires it?

Is there a periodic reconciliation between the current organizational structure or role assignments and the training matrix, so that people moved into new roles promptly receive the new role's required training?

sections.name: Complaints, Recalls, Returns & Mandatory Reporting
sections.intro: An investigator will test whether you capture every product complaint, evaluate it for a quality defect and a possible adverse event, investigate when warranted, and feed signals into your quality system. They will also probe your ability to remove product from the market fast (recall plus mock recalls), control returned and salvaged goods, and meet hard regulatory clocks for Field Alert Reports, Biological Product Deviation Reports, and shortage notifications.

questions.q: Do you have an approved SOP defining a complaint, the intake channels (phone, email, sales rep, distributor, portal), and a single owner accountable for the complaint system, with all channels funneling into one controlled complaint file?

Is every complaint, including verbal ones, logged with a unique identifier on the same day it is received, so an investigator can reconcile your complaint log against call logs, emails, and sales contacts without finding unrecorded complaints?

Does your written record for each complaint capture the required elements under 211.198(b) (name and strength of the drug, dosage form, lot or control number, name of the complainant, nature of the complaint, and the reply to the complainant)?

Are all complaints reviewed by the quality unit, with a documented decision and rationale for whether an investigation under 211.192 is needed, and is the reason recorded when no investigation is performed?

When a complaint involves a possible failure of a drug to meet any of its specifications, do you investigate it and, where the lot has been distributed, extend the review to other potentially affected lots and prior periods per 211.192?

Does every complaint investigation reach a documented root cause (or a justified conclusion that none could be found), assess the same defect in other batches, and link to CAPA where a corrective action is warranted?

Do you have defined timeliness targets for complaint acknowledgement, investigation, and closure, and do you trend on-time-closure performance and aging open complaints with escalation for overdue cases?

Is there a documented procedure and decision tree to screen every complaint for a reportable adverse event, and a defined handoff that routes safety information to pharmacovigilance or drug safety within a timeframe that protects expedited (15-day) and periodic reporting clocks?
Can you demonstrate that the quality complaint file and the safety (adverse event) database are reconciled, so an event reported through one is not lost to the other, with no orphaned safety reports?

For complaints that involve a returned sample or physical evidence, do you control receipt, identification, quarantine, and testing of the returned unit, and retain it appropriately while the investigation is open?
Do you trend complaints by product, defect type, and lot on a defined cycle, set action thresholds, and feed the trends into management review and the Annual Product Review or Product Quality Review?

Where a contract manufacturer, packager, or distributor receives complaints on your product, does the quality agreement require timely transfer of those complaints to you, and can you show evidence those complaints actually reach your complaint system?

Do you have a written recall procedure that defines recall classes (Class I, II, III), the recall committee and decision authority, communication with FDA, and the steps to initiate, conduct, and terminate a recall?

Does your recall procedure define and assign a recall coordinator and a 24/7 contact, and identify when and how you would notify your FDA district or CDER/CBER, consistent with 21 CFR Part 7 recall guidance?

Do you perform documented mock recalls on a defined frequency that test both upstream traceability (component and material to finished lot) and downstream traceability (finished lot to consignee), with a measured recovery percentage and time-to-reconcile?

Do your mock recalls reconcile quantity manufactured against quantity distributed, on-hand, returned, and accounted for, and do you have a target reconciliation percentage with investigation when it is not met?

Is your distribution record system able to produce, by lot, a complete list of direct consignees with contact details and quantities shipped, fast enough to support a Class I recall, per 211.196 distribution records (and 211.150 distribution procedures designed to facilitate recall)?

Do you have a procedure to draft, approve, and issue recall communications (recall notices, press releases, effectiveness checks) and to track consignee responses to a defined effectiveness-check level?
When a recall is closed, do you document the disposition of recalled product, an effectiveness assessment, CAPA from root cause, and a recall termination request to FDA where applicable?

Do you have a procedure governing receipt of returned drug products that controls identification, segregation, and quarantine, and prohibits return to salable stock without a documented quality disposition?
For returned goods, do you assess and document the storage and shipping conditions the product was exposed to before the return per 21 CFR 211.204?

If you reprocess, relabel, or return any returned product to stock, is there supporting evidence (testing, examination, storage history) and a quality-unit approved disposition record justifying that the product still meets specifications?

Do you have a salvaging procedure that prohibits salvaging drug products exposed to improper conditions (fire, flood, accident, adverse storage) unless evidence shows the product meets all applicable standards under 211.208?

Do you reconcile returned and salvaged quantities against distribution and complaint data so a return that signals a quality problem (multiple returns of the same lot) triggers an investigation rather than just restocking or destruction?

Do you have a written procedure that defines the three categories of Field Alert Report under 21 CFR 314.81(b)(1) (mix-ups, bacterial contamination/significant chemical-physical-or-other change-or-deterioration in the distributed drug, and failure of one or more distributed batches to meet specification) and the 3-working-day clock?

Can you demonstrate you submit Field Alert Reports within 3 working days of becoming aware of the information, and that an initial FAR is filed on time even when the investigation is not yet complete, with follow-up FARs to update or close it?

Is there a clear, documented linkage so that a complaint, OOS, or stability failure on a distributed lot is evaluated for FAR reportability, with the evaluation and the not-reportable rationale captured?

Do you maintain a log of all FARs submitted (and decisions not to submit) with dates, the triggering event, the lot involved, follow-ups, and closure, so an investigator can audit your FAR timeliness and completeness?

For licensed biological products, do you have a procedure to file Biological Product Deviation Reports under 21 CFR 600.14 within 45 calendar days for events that may affect safety, purity, or potency of distributed product, and do you track that clock?

Can you distinguish which deviations on a biological product require a BPDR (distributed product) versus internal-only documentation, and is responsibility for the BPDR clearly assigned (manufacturer who had control when the deviation occurred)?

Do you have a procedure to assess potential or actual supply disruptions and to notify FDA of a permanent discontinuance or interruption in manufacturing under the drug shortage reporting requirements (FD&C Act 506C), within the required notification window?

For applicable products, do you maintain a current list of which products are subject to mandatory shortage notification (including life-supporting, life-sustaining, and products for serious or debilitating conditions) and a trigger to evaluate notification when a quality event or recall threatens supply?

Are personnel who handle intake, triage, investigation, and reporting trained and current on the complaint, recall, returns, FAR, BPDR, and shortage procedures, with training records that map to their assigned roles?

Do management review and the quality system receive metrics on complaints, recalls, mock-recall recovery, returns, FAR/BPDR timeliness, and shortage events, with documented escalation of adverse trends to senior management?

If complaint, recall, or reporting workflows run in a computerized system, is that system validated, access-controlled, and audit-trailed so reportability decisions, dates, and submissions cannot be altered without an attributable record?

For combination products, do you have a procedure addressing the combination-product postmarket safety reporting requirements under 21 CFR Part 4 Subpart B, including five-day, malfunction, and other device-side reports in addition to drug-side reporting?

Do you periodically test your reporting readiness end to end (for example through a tabletop or mock event) to confirm that intake, escalation, decision, and on-time submission of a FAR, BPDR, or shortage notice would actually happen if the clock started today?

sections.name: Annual Product Review, Management Review, Metrics & Inspection Readiness
sections.intro: An inspector expects every marketed product to receive a documented annual review that actually evaluates trends and drives action, senior management to review the quality system on a defined cadence with decisions recorded, a working internal audit and self-inspection program, and an organization that can run an inspection in a controlled way and respond to observations on time.

questions.q: Is an Annual Product Review (APR) or Product Quality Review (PQR) prepared for every marketed drug product at a defined frequency of at least once every 12 months, with a written procedure defining its content, owner, and approval route?
Does each APR/PQR cover all batches produced in the review period, including rejected, reprocessed, and reworked batches, rather than a sample of batches?
Does the APR review a representative number of batches, whether approved or rejected, and the records of those batches, as required by 21 CFR 211.180(e)?

Does the APR evaluate the quality standards of each drug product to determine the need for changes to specifications, manufacturing instructions, or control procedures, and is that conclusion stated explicitly?

Are critical in-process and finished-product test results trended in the APR (for example assay, dissolution, impurities, microbial limits) with the actual data shown, not just a statement that results were within specification?

Does the APR include a review of all deviations, nonconformances, OOS and OOT results, and the investigations associated with the product, with an assessment of whether trends point to a systemic issue?
Does the APR summarize and assess all changes made to the product, process, equipment, methods, and suppliers during the period, and confirm those changes were made under change control?
Does the APR review returned product, complaints, recalls, and Field Alert Reports for the product and link them back to potential product or process causes?
Does the APR include a review of stability data and any stability trends or failures, and confirm the adequacy of the current expiry or retest dating?
For the period under review, does the APR confirm whether the marketed product remained within the validated state, including a review of CPV or process capability data where available?
Does the APR explicitly review starting material and packaging material quality, including changes to suppliers, supplier-related rejections, and out-of-specification incoming materials?
Does the APR review the adequacy of corrective actions taken for product or process issues during the prior period and confirm those actions were effective?

Do APR conclusions generate documented follow-up actions (CAPA, change controls, or specification reviews) with owners and due dates, rather than ending with a generic statement that no action is required?
Are APRs completed within the timeframe defined in your procedure, with no chronically overdue reviews?

Where products are manufactured at multiple sites or by a contract manufacturer, does the APR consolidate data across all sites and define which party owns and approves the review per the quality agreement?

Is there a documented management review of the pharmaceutical quality system held on a defined periodic schedule, with senior management participation recorded by name and role, consistent with ICH Q10?

Does the management review consider defined inputs such as quality metrics, deviation and CAPA status, complaint and recall trends, audit results, regulatory inspection outcomes, and the status of prior management review actions?
Does management review include the performance and effectiveness of the quality system itself, including assessment of process performance and product quality monitoring outputs?
Are management review outputs captured as documented decisions and actions with assigned owners, due dates, and resource commitments, and are those actions tracked to closure?
Does management review explicitly evaluate the adequacy of resources, including staffing, facilities, and systems, to support current and planned operations?
Is there a defined set of quality metrics or KPIs with documented definitions, calculation methods, data sources, and targets or alert and action thresholds?

Do your quality metrics include measures of recurrence and effectiveness (for example repeat deviations, CAPA effectiveness failures, right-first-time, batch rejection rate, invalidated OOS rate) rather than only counts of records opened and closed?
Are quality metrics trended over time and escalated to management when an alert or action limit is breached, with the escalation and any resulting action documented?
Can you demonstrate that quality metrics drive decisions, for example by tracing a metric trend to a specific management review action, CAPA, or resource change?
Is there a documented internal audit or self-inspection program with a risk-based schedule covering all GMP areas, systems, and applicable sites over a defined cycle?
Are internal audits conducted by trained, qualified auditors who are independent of the area being audited, with their qualification and independence documented?
Are self-inspections actually performed on schedule, and can you show that planned audits for the current and prior cycle were completed and are not chronically deferred?
Are internal audit findings closed with documented CAPA, and is the effectiveness of those corrective actions verified after closure?
Are internal audit results and the status of resulting actions reported to senior management as a defined input to management review?
Does your supplier and contract manufacturer audit program operate as part of the same controlled process, with audit frequency justified on a risk basis and findings tracked to closure?

Is there an inspection readiness or inspection management procedure defining roles, front room and back room responsibilities, the document request process, and rules for what is and is not provided to investigators?
Are key staff trained on inspection behavior and roles, including SME presentation, scribe and runner duties, and the practice of answering only the question asked, with that training documented?

Can the back room retrieve requested records, including batch records, validation reports, training records, and electronic data with audit trails, within a defined and reasonable time during an inspection?
Is there a defined process to log, review, and quality-check every document before it is handed to an investigator, and to maintain a master list of everything provided during the inspection?

Do you maintain a current inspection history file with prior FDA Form 483s, Establishment Inspection Reports, warning letters, and your responses, and can you demonstrate that prior commitments were actually implemented?

Is there a procedure governing the response to an FDA Form 483 that targets a timely written response (FDA generally expects within 15 business days) addressing corrections, corrective actions, and timelines for each observation?

Do 483 and warning letter responses address not only the specific observation but also a documented assessment of whether the same issue exists elsewhere (scope and systemic evaluation) and the steps to prevent recurrence?
Are commitments made in inspection responses tracked as formal CAPAs to verified completion and effectiveness?

For sterile or aseptically processed products, does the annual review specifically trend environmental monitoring, media fill (aseptic process simulation), and sterility assurance data, and feed adverse trends into the contamination control strategy and management review?

For biological products, does the product review include lot release testing trends, potency, and any comparability or stability signals, and is it consistent with 21 CFR 600 series record and reporting expectations?

For cell and gene therapy or short shelf-life products, is there a defined approach to periodic product review and metrics that accounts for small batch numbers, autologous or patient-specific lots, and chain of identity and custody trends?

For combination products, does the periodic review and metrics program cover each constituent part per the streamlined approach in 21 CFR Part 4 (drug and, where present, device requirements), including device-related complaints and any reportable events?

For computerized systems supporting APR, metrics, and management review, are the data sources validated, and can you demonstrate the reported numbers are complete and accurate against the underlying records?

sections.name: Biologics-Specific Controls
sections.intro: An inspector in this area is testing whether the biological controls unique to a licensed biologic hold up: that cell banks are fully characterized and qualified, that viral and adventitious-agent safety is built into the process with validated clearance, that biological raw materials are controlled and traceable, that potency and lot release testing actually measure what they claim, and that any change has been shown comparable before it reached product. They will trace a banked vial through characterization, follow a lot from raw material receipt to release, and probe how you proved a change did not alter the molecule.

questions.q: Is there a documented two-tiered cell banking system (Master Cell Bank and Working Cell Bank, or a single-tier system with documented justification) with defined generation, qualification, and use of each tier?
Are the history and origin of the cell substrate documented, including the source organism, parental cell line, derivation history, and any genetic modification or vector construction?
Is the Master Cell Bank characterized per ICH Q5A, Q5B, and Q5D for identity, purity, genetic stability, and freedom from adventitious agents (bacteria, fungi, mycoplasma, and relevant viruses)?

For recombinant products, has genetic stability and consistency of the expression construct been confirmed (for example by nucleic acid sequencing or restriction mapping) at the cell bank level and at or beyond the limit of in vitro cell age used for production?
Has a ceiling been set for how many times the production cells may be passaged or doubled, and is that ceiling backed by data showing the cells stay stable up to it?
Are cell banks (master and working) stored under controlled, monitored conditions with documented, traceable inventory control?
Is there a documented procedure governing how new Working Cell Banks are generated from the Master Cell Bank, including the qualification testing each new WCB must pass before use?

Has the manufacturing process been assessed for viral safety across all three angles expected by ICH Q5A: screening the cell lines and input materials, measuring how well the process removes or inactivates virus, and testing the product itself at suitable steps?

Has viral clearance been demonstrated through validated spiking studies on scaled-down models, with at least two orthogonal clearance steps and clearance factors calculated for relevant and model viruses?
Are the scaled-down models used in viral clearance studies documented as representative of the manufacturing scale, and are the spiking virus stocks characterized?

Is unprocessed bulk (or a comparable in-process material) tested for adventitious agents at an appropriate stage, including in vitro and in vivo adventitious virus assays as applicable to the product type?
Is mycoplasma testing performed at defined points (for example on cell banks and at the production bioreactor or harvest stage) using a validated method?
For products using bovine-derived materials, is there documentation showing the materials meet TSE/BSE control expectations (country of origin sourcing, certificates) per the applicable guidance?

Are all raw materials of biological or animal origin (serum, trypsin, growth factors, hydrolysates, enzymes) identified, risk-assessed for adventitious agents, and supported by supplier documentation including certificates of analysis and origin?

Where animal-origin materials have been replaced with recombinant or chemically defined alternatives, is the change documented and any residual animal-origin material in the process still controlled and traced?
Is there full lot-to-lot traceability linking each finished biologic lot back through in-process materials, raw material lots of biological origin, and the specific cell bank vial used?
Is a validated, stability-indicating potency assay in place that measures the relevant biological activity of the product, with a defined acceptance range, per 21 CFR 610.10?

Has the potency assay (and its reference standard) been qualified, with system suitability criteria, assay controls, and a defined strategy for trending assay performance and reference standard bridging?

Is there a qualified primary or working reference standard, with documented characterization, a defined replacement and re-qualification protocol, and traceability to the original characterized material?
Does lot release testing include the applicable general biological product standards under 21 CFR 610 (sterility per 610.12, purity per 610.13, and identity per 610.14)?
Is finished product sterility testing performed per a validated method (and is any use of rapid or alternative sterility methods supported by validation and a regulatory filing where required)?
Are endotoxin/pyrogen limits established and tested for each lot per 21 CFR 610.13 using a validated method appropriate to the product?
Is product identity confirmed by a specific test that distinguishes the product from other products made in the same facility per 21 CFR 610.14?

Are process-related and product-related impurities (for example host cell protein, host cell and vector DNA, aggregates, and process additives) characterized, controlled to defined limits, and tested by validated methods?
For products requiring it, are residual host cell DNA quantity and size controlled to limits consistent with current regulatory expectations?

Is a formal comparability protocol or strategy used to evaluate the impact of any manufacturing change (process, scale, site, raw material, cell bank) following ICH Q5E, with predefined acceptance criteria?
When quality-attribute data alone cannot establish comparability after a manufacturing change, is nonclinical or clinical bridging data evaluated per ICH Q5E?

Are characterization studies (higher-order structure, glycosylation, charge variants, and other product-specific attributes) available to support comparability and characterization beyond routine release tests?

Is there a documented stability program for drug substance and drug product, including stability-indicating methods, that supports the labeled shelf life and storage conditions and includes any post-change stability bridging?

Are out-of-specification and out-of-trend results on biological release and stability tests investigated, with potency and adventitious-agent failures escalated and assessed for batch and patient impact?

Are changes to the cell bank, the production process, or biological raw materials filed with the agency at the correct reporting category (prior approval supplement, CBE-30, CBE-0, or annual report) before or as required by the change?

Is process consistency demonstrated and maintained through process performance qualification and ongoing process verification, including trending of critical quality attributes such as potency, purity, and impurity profiles across lots?
For cell and gene therapy products, are donor eligibility and material qualification controlled per 21 CFR 1271 (where applicable) in addition to the 600-series biologics requirements?

For cell and gene therapy products, are vector identity, vector copy number, replication-competent virus (for example RCR/RCL), and transgene expression tested as part of characterization and release?

For autologous or short-shelf-life cell therapies, is there a defined and validated approach to lot release that handles rapid testing, conditional release where used, and chain of identity and chain of custody from donor to patient?

Are reagents and ancillary materials used in cell and gene therapy manufacturing (cytokines, beads, media, viral vectors) qualified, risk-tiered, and controlled with documented removal or residual limits where they are not intended to remain in the product?
Are records related to biological products (cell bank generation, viral safety, lot release) retained for the period required under 21 CFR 600/211 and readily retrievable for inspection?

Are the people performing biological characterization, viral testing, potency, and release testing trained and qualified, with methods validated or qualified for their intended use under 21 CFR 211.165(e)?

sections.name: Cell & Gene Therapy / ATMP & Tissue (HCT/Ps)
sections.intro: An inspector in this area expects to see end-to-end control of living, patient-specific or donor-derived material: documented donor eligibility, an unbroken chain of identity and custody from collection to administration, aseptic processing under proper classification, validated potency and vector controls, and release decisions that hold up under short shelf-life pressure. Expect questions probing 21 CFR Part 1271 (registration, donor eligibility, current good tissue practice), the cGMP overlay for licensed products, and the special handling of autologous and short-dated material.

questions.q: Have you determined and documented the correct regulatory framework for each product (HCT/P regulated solely under Section 361 and Part 1271, versus a 351 product requiring an IND/BLA plus cGMP), and does your quality system reflect that determination?
If you handle HCT/Ps, are your establishment functions registered and your products listed with FDA per 21 CFR 1271 Subpart B, with the registration kept current as functions or product types change?

Is donor eligibility determined and documented by a responsible person for every donor before the cells or tissue are made available for use, with both donor screening and donor testing completed per Subpart C (1271.50 through 1271.90)?

Does donor screening include a documented review of relevant medical records for risk factors and clinical evidence of relevant communicable disease agents and diseases, including the specific risk factors and conditions listed in current FDA donor-eligibility guidance?

Is donor testing performed using FDA-licensed, approved, or cleared donor screening assays run by an appropriately certified (e.g., CLIA) laboratory, with the panel of communicable disease agents tested determined by the cell or tissue type per 21 CFR 1271.85?

For donors of viable, leukocyte-rich cells or tissue, do you test for relevant agents using specimens collected within the timeframe required by 1271.80, and apply the correct rules for living versus deceased donors?

When an HCT/P from a donor determined ineligible or with incomplete eligibility is made available for use under an exception (e.g., autologous use per 1271.90), is the required documentation and labeling of the ineligibility/incompleteness in place per 1271.65 and 1271.90?

For autologous products, do you still maintain the donor-eligibility records and the 1271.90 exception documentation, and do you have positive identity linkage proving the cells returned to the patient are that patient's own cells?

Is there a documented chain of identity that uniquely links the patient or donor to the collected material, every in-process intermediate, and the final product, using a unique identifier that is carried on labels and records at every step?

Is the chain of custody documented as an unbroken record of who had control of the material at each transfer, with date, time, condition, and verification of identity at apheresis collection, shipping, receipt, manufacturing, release, distribution, and administration?

Are independent two-person or system-enforced identity verification checks (for example label-to-patient-to-product reconciliation) performed and recorded at the critical chain-of-identity hand-offs, especially collection and administration?

Is there a validated mix-up prevention strategy that physically and procedurally segregates patient-specific batches so that two different patients' materials cannot be processed in the same space at the same time without controls preventing cross-contamination or label swap?

Are the chain-of-identity and chain-of-custody systems (including any vein-to-vein tracking or order management software) validated, access-controlled, and audit-trailed so the patient linkage cannot be silently altered?

Is aseptic processing of cells performed in an appropriately classified environment (ISO 5 critical zone within an ISO 7 background, or a validated closed/functionally closed system), with the classification justified by a contamination control strategy?
Are open manipulations of the cell product minimized, and where open steps remain, are they performed in a unidirectional-airflow ISO 5 zone with documented smoke studies and qualified operators?

Do you run aseptic process simulations (media fills) that represent the actual manual manipulations, hold times, number of interventions, and worst-case conditions of your cell process, including for each operator who performs aseptic steps, at the required frequency?

Is environmental monitoring (viable and nonviable particulates, surfaces, and personnel) performed during processing with defined alert and action limits, trended, and tied to documented investigations and product impact assessments when exceeded?

Are operators who perform aseptic manipulations qualified through gowning qualification, aseptic technique assessment, and successful participation in media fills, with requalification on a defined schedule?

Because the final product cannot be terminally sterilized, do you have a defined microbial control program that includes in-process bioburden, endotoxin testing, and a sterility method appropriate to short shelf-life material?

For products released before final sterility results, is there a written procedure defining the action to take (including physician/patient notification and recall consideration) if a sterility positive is detected after the product has been administered?
Is there a documented, risk-based release strategy for short shelf-life products that defines which tests must be complete before release versus reported post-administration?
Are conditional or real-time release decisions made and signed by the quality unit, with the basis for accepting incomplete data documented per batch rather than as a blanket practice?

Do you have a validated, biologically relevant potency assay (or a matrix of complementary assays) that measures the product's relevant biological activity or mechanism of action, with defined acceptance criteria per 21 CFR 610.10?

If your potency assay is still maturing, do you have a defined potency strategy and lifecycle plan agreed with FDA, with surrogate measures and a path to a quantitative, mechanism-relevant assay before licensure?
Is the potency assay qualified or validated for accuracy, precision, specificity, and a suitable reference standard, with assay controls and system suitability that detect a non-potent batch?

Do you characterize and control the cellular composition of the product (identity, viability, cell count, phenotype, and impurity or residual non-target cell levels) with release specifications for each critical quality attribute?

For gene-modified products, is the viral or non-viral vector manufactured under cGMP with controls over identity, titer or copy number, purity, replication-competent virus (RCR/RCL) testing, and removal of process-related impurities such as residual host-cell DNA and protein?

Is vector copy number (or transgene integration/expression) measured per batch against a defined specification, and is there testing for replication-competent vector at the stages FDA guidance expects (vector lot, transduced cells, and where applicable patient follow-up)?

Are the cell banks and viral seed/banks (master and working) fully characterized, tested for adventitious agents, sterility, mycoplasma, and identity, and stored under controlled, monitored, and redundant conditions?

Are raw and ancillary materials of biological origin (serum, cytokines, enzymes, antibodies, beads) qualified for adventitious-agent safety and traceability, with a strategy to demonstrate removal or acceptable residual levels of any reagent that should not be in the final product?

Are critical reagents and single-use components qualified, lot-controlled, and traceable to each patient batch, with extractables/leachables assessed for the contact materials used in processing and final container?

Is cryopreservation and the cold chain controlled and validated, with continuous temperature monitoring, qualified shipping containers, defined excursion limits, and documented disposition rules for temperature excursions at any point from collection to administration?
Are hold times, thaw procedures, and post-thaw stability defined and validated so the product administered to the patient still meets viability and potency at the point of use?

Do you have validated stability data supporting the assigned shelf-life and storage conditions for the final product and key intermediates, including a stability-indicating profile for short-dated material?

Is process validation appropriate for low-volume, patient-specific batches in place, recognizing that traditional three-lot validation may be replaced by a justified strategy combining process characterization, control strategy, and ongoing verification?

Are deviations, out-of-specification results, and process failures for individual patient batches investigated with the same rigor as commercial batches, including impact on the specific patient and timely notification to the treating physician when the product is already in use?

Are HCT/P adverse reactions and product deviations reported to FDA within the timeframes of 21 CFR 1271.350 (1271.350(a) adverse reaction reporting and 1271.350(b) HCT/P deviation reporting), and for licensed (351) products, are biological product deviation reports filed per 21 CFR 600.14?

Do you maintain records that allow tracking of each unit from the donor or patient to the consignee and back, for the retention period required by 1271.55 and 1271.290, so that any safety signal can be traced in both directions?

Is there a documented procedure to recover, recall, or quarantine product across the supply chain (including material already shipped to a treatment site) given the patient-specific and time-critical nature of these products?

sections.name: Active Pharmaceutical Ingredient (API) GMP
sections.intro: An inspector assessing API manufacturing under ICH Q7 expects to see a clear, justified point where GMP controls begin (the API starting material), documented process controls and impurity limits tied to the validated process, controlled batch production and review records, and a change management system that evaluates impact on quality before any change is made. Use these checks to find where the GMP trail thins out.

questions.q: Have you defined and documented the point at which API starting materials are introduced into the process, with the GMP controls of ICH Q7 applied from that step forward and a written rationale for that designation?
Is the designation of each API starting material justified by an evaluation of its impact on the impurity profile of the final API, rather than chosen for convenience or to limit GMP scope?

For API starting materials, intermediates, and raw materials, do you have approved specifications and qualify suppliers against them, including specifications for materials that affect critical quality attributes?

Are incoming API starting materials and key raw materials identity-tested and released against specification before use, with any reliance on a supplier certificate of analysis backed by periodic full re-test and an initial supplier qualification?
Have you identified the critical process parameters and critical quality attributes for each API step, with established acceptable ranges supported by process development and validation data?
Are in-process controls and in-process testing defined at the steps where they matter, with action and acceptance limits set, monitored, and trended over time?

Is process validation performed on the commercial API process using a defined number of consecutive successful batches, with a written protocol, predetermined acceptance criteria, and a final report, before routine release of validated product?
Do you have an ongoing program (continued process verification or periodic review) to confirm the API process stays in a state of control after initial validation?

Have you established the impurity profile for each API, including organic impurities, inorganic impurities, residual solvents, and where relevant elemental impurities and mutagenic impurities, and do you compare each batch profile against that established profile?

Are specified, unspecified, and total impurity limits set with scientific and toxicological justification (for example consistent with ICH Q3A, Q3C residual solvents, Q3D elemental impurities, and M7 for mutagenic impurities where applicable)?

When a new impurity appears or an impurity exceeds its established limit, is it investigated, characterized or identified at the appropriate threshold, and assessed for safety impact before the batch is dispositioned?

Are carryover of impurities and cross-contamination between products controlled, with cleaning validation that accounts for the most difficult-to-clean and most toxic residues, and limits justified to protect the next product?

Do API batch production records reproduce the relevant master production instruction, capture each significant step as performed with actual quantities and equipment used, and identify the operators and verifiers by signature and date?
Are unique batch or lot numbers assigned so that the complete history of each API batch, including the starting materials and intermediates that fed it, can be traced forward and backward?

Is each API batch record reviewed and approved by the quality unit before the batch is released, with all deviations, out-of-specification results, and in-process anomalies resolved and documented at that point?
Are actual yields at significant steps recorded and compared against expected yields, with discrepancies outside established limits investigated?

Does your change management system require that proposed changes to raw materials, equipment, process steps, specifications, test methods, or facilities be written up, evaluated for quality impact, and approved by the quality unit before implementation?
For each significant change, do you assess whether revalidation, additional stability data, or notification to the regulatory authority is needed, and document that determination?
After a change is implemented, do you evaluate the first batches produced under the change to confirm the expected quality and impurity profile were maintained?

Is the quality unit independent of production, with defined responsibility and authority to release or reject all API batches, intermediates, raw materials, and packaging, and to approve or reject changes, deviations, and specifications?

Do you conduct a periodic product quality review for each API that examines batch results, deviations, out-of-specification results, changes, stability trends, and returns or complaints, with conclusions and any required actions documented?

Is a deviation and CAPA system in place that classifies deviations, drives investigations to root cause for significant events, and tracks corrective and preventive actions to closure with effectiveness assessment?

Are out-of-specification and out-of-trend results investigated under a defined procedure that distinguishes laboratory error from a true result before any retest or resampling, with batch disposition based on the investigation conclusion?

Is a stability program in place using validated stability-indicating methods, with at least one batch per year placed on study (where production occurs), to establish or confirm retest dates or expiry dates for the API?
Are retest dates or expiry dates assigned to each API batch and supported by stability data, with materials re-evaluated or re-tested before use once the retest date has passed?

Are analytical methods used for API release and stability validated (or verified for compendial methods) for the intended use, including specificity, accuracy, precision, linearity, range, and sensitivity as applicable?

Is laboratory data captured under good documentation practices and data integrity controls, with audit trails reviewed, no untracked reprocessing of chromatographic or other electronic data, and clear traceability from raw data to reported result?

Are reprocessing and reworking of API batches controlled by procedure, justified, evaluated for impact on quality and impurity profile, approved by the quality unit, and supported by data showing equivalent quality, with reworking handled under a deviation or change record?

Is recovery of solvents, mother liquors, and reagents controlled by procedure so that recovered materials meet specifications appropriate for the step where they are reused, with their use documented and recoveries that could affect the impurity profile evaluated?

Are equipment and utilities (including water and process gases) qualified and maintained, with cleaning between batches and between products controlled, and dedicated equipment used where required to prevent contamination?

Is microbial and endotoxin control applied where the API quality or its intended use requires it (for example sterile or low-bioburden APIs), with appropriate environmental, water, and in-process monitoring?
Are intermediates and APIs stored, packaged, and labeled under conditions that protect their quality, with label controls that prevent mixups and reconciliation of issued labels?

Is the chain of custody and quality oversight maintained when steps are outsourced to a contract manufacturer or laboratory, governed by a written quality agreement that defines GMP responsibilities and supported by audits of the contractor?
Do you maintain accurate distribution records for each API batch that enable a recall, and is a recall procedure established that can be initiated and executed in a timely way?

Are agents, brokers, traders, distributors, repackers, and relabelers in your supply chain held to ICH Q7 expectations, including transfer of original certificates of analysis, traceability to the original manufacturer, and no obscuring of the API origin?
Are personnel performing and overseeing API manufacture and testing trained and qualified for their specific tasks, with training records current and competency confirmed?

sections.name: Combination Products
sections.intro: An inspector or sharp auditor looking at combination products expects to see that the company has correctly classified the product, identified its primary mode of action, decided which CGMP framework applies under 21 CFR Part 4, and then actually built the required device-side controls (design controls, CAPA, complaint and adverse-event handling, purchasing controls) on top of its drug or biologic quality system, or the reverse. They will probe how the streamlined approach was implemented in practice, whether human factors and use-related risk were addressed, and whether the constituent parts, the combination, and any cross-labeled or third-party arrangements are all controlled.

questions.q: Have you formally determined and documented whether each marketed product is a combination product under 21 CFR 3.2(e), including the specific combination type (single-entity, co-packaged, or cross-labeled)?
Is the primary mode of action (PMOA) for each combination product documented with a written rationale, and does it correctly identify the lead constituent part (drug, biologic, or device)?

If the PMOA was unclear and you used a Request for Designation (RFD) or pre-RFD to obtain an Agency assignment of the lead center, is that determination on file and reflected in your regulatory and quality strategy?

Have you decided, and documented in a quality system procedure, whether you operate under a drug CGMP base with added device provisions or a device QMS base with added drug provisions per 21 CFR 4.4 (the streamlined approach)?

If you operate a drug or biologic CGMP base (21 CFR 210/211 or 600-series), can you demonstrate compliance with the specifically called-out device provisions in 21 CFR 4.4(b)(1): design controls 820.30, purchasing controls 820.50, CAPA 820.100, installation 820.170, and servicing 820.200?

If you operate under a device QMS base per 21 CFR 4.4(b)(2), do you also demonstrate compliance with the specifically called-out drug CGMP provisions that apply to your product (211.84, 211.103, 211.132, 211.137, 211.165, 211.166-211.167, 211.170)?
Have you mapped each applicable CGMP requirement to the specific procedure, record, or control that satisfies it, so that an inspector can trace any Part 4 provision to objective evidence?

Where you rely on a single quality system to satisfy overlapping drug and device requirements (for example CAPA versus complaint handling, or design controls versus drug development), is the bridging logic documented so it is clear no requirement was dropped?

For the device constituent part, do you maintain design controls under 820.30 including design and development planning, design inputs, design outputs, design review, design verification, design validation, design transfer, design changes, and a design history file?

Are user needs and intended uses translated into documented design inputs that include device performance, delivery accuracy or dose, and use environment for the combination product as a whole, not just the device alone?
Has design verification confirmed that the design outputs meet the design inputs for the device constituent part?

Is there a current, complete design history file (DHF) for the device constituent, and a device master record (DMR) or equivalent that defines the specifications and procedures for the constituent part?

Have you applied use-related risk analysis and human factors / usability engineering to the combination product, consistent with applicable FDA human factors guidance, for products with a user interface (for example autoinjectors, on-body injectors, inhalers, pens)?

Have you identified critical tasks (tasks where use error could cause serious harm) and confirmed through human factors validation testing with representative users that the product can be used safely and effectively as intended?
Are use errors and use-related hazards captured in your risk management file (consistent with ISO 14971) and linked to design mitigations, labeling, and instructions for use?
Do your instructions for use (IFU), Quick Reference Guides, and labeling reflect the validated use steps, and were they evaluated in the human factors study rather than written after the fact?

Does your CAPA system under 820.100 cover the device constituent and the combination product as a whole, with quality data analysis, root cause investigation, effectiveness checks, and links to complaint and adverse-event data?

Do purchasing controls under 820.50 apply to suppliers of the device constituent and critical components (for example the delivery device, container closure with a device function), including documented supplier evaluation, defined purchasing data, and acceptance activities?

Where installation or servicing applies to the device constituent (for example a reusable injector or pump), do you meet 820.170 and 820.200, including service report handling and analysis as a quality data source?

For the drug or biologic constituent, do you have stability data supporting the assigned expiration dating of the combination product, accounting for any interaction between the drug and the device materials (extractables, leachables, adsorption)?

Have you established and validated container closure integrity and, where the device is the container closure (prefilled syringe, cartridge), confirmed compatibility and functional performance across shelf life?
Is dose accuracy, delivered dose, or delivery performance defined as a release specification and confirmed at release and on stability where the device meters or delivers the drug?

Have you handled post-market safety reporting correctly under 21 CFR Part 4 Subpart B (21 CFR 4.100-4.126), determining the reporting obligations that flow from the constituent parts and the combination as a whole?

Do you submit the post-market safety reports required under the combination product PMSR framework (21 CFR 4.100-4.126), with each report type (ICSRs, malfunction reports, five-day reports) triggered and timed per its own underlying requirement?

Is your complaint handling system able to identify whether a complaint concerns the drug constituent, the device constituent, or the combination, and to route it to the correct investigation and reporting path?

Do you have a procedure that defines the post-market reporting timelines and decision logic so that a malfunction that could cause or contribute to death or serious injury is captured as a reportable event?

Where the combination product is co-packaged or cross-labeled, is there a written agreement or quality agreement with the other party that defines responsibilities for design, manufacturing, complaint handling, adverse-event reporting, and changes affecting the combined product?
For cross-labeled combination products, can each party demonstrate awareness of and access to the information needed to meet its own CGMP and reporting obligations for the combined use?

When the device constituent or its manufacturing is performed by a contract manufacturer or device supplier, have you defined which party is responsible for which Part 4 provisions (design controls, DHF, complaint and MDR-type reporting) and verified it through oversight?

Do your change control procedures evaluate whether a change to the drug, the device, the IFU, or the manufacturing process affects the other constituent, the PMOA assignment, design validation, human factors validation, or stability?
Is there a defined process for assessing when a change to the combination product requires a new regulatory submission or supplement?

Have you confirmed that personnel working on the combination product are trained on both the drug or biologic CGMP requirements and the device requirements that apply to their roles, rather than only one framework?
Do internal audits and management review explicitly cover the Part 4 combination-product controls (the called-out device or drug provisions, human factors, PMSR), not only the base quality system?

Can you produce a single document or matrix that an investigator can use to see the product classification, PMOA, lead center, chosen CGMP operating model, and the controls satisfying each cross-applied requirement?

For sterile or aseptically filled combination products (for example prefilled syringes filled aseptically), are the sterility assurance, environmental monitoring, and aseptic process controls integrated with the device assembly and functional testing controls?

For biologic-led combination products, have you confirmed that the 600-series biologics requirements are met alongside the called-out device provisions, including lot release and any product-specific license commitments?

For a constituent part that includes software (a connected injector, a dose-logging app, or an electromechanical delivery device), have you applied software lifecycle controls and software validation appropriate to its risk, consistent with the device design controls?

Have you defined acceptance activities and incoming, in-process, and finished-product testing that confirm both the drug attributes and the device functional attributes before the combination product is released?

sections.name: Clinical Quality / GCP
sections.intro: An investigator or sharp internal auditor in this area is testing whether the sponsor actually exercises oversight of the trial rather than delegating and forgetting it: a documented quality system, risk-based monitoring with evidence the issues found were acted on, a complete and contemporaneous trial master file, validated electronic systems with intact audit trails, controlled handling of deviations, blinding and unblinding, and safety reporting that meets timelines. The questions below probe the specifics that distinguish a compliant clinical operation from one that merely has procedures on paper.

questions.q: Do you have a documented clinical quality management system that defines roles, responsibilities, and processes for trial conduct, and is it built around the quality-by-design and risk-based principles in ICH E6(R3) rather than only a collection of standalone SOPs?

For each trial, have you identified and documented the factors most important to quality (the data and processes that most affect participant safety and the reliability of the results) and focused your quality and oversight activities on them?

Do you maintain a documented risk assessment for each trial that identifies risks to participants and data, assigns likelihood and impact, defines mitigations, and is reviewed and updated as the trial progresses?

When activities are delegated to a CRO or other service provider, is the delegation captured in a written agreement that specifies exactly which sponsor obligations are transferred and which the sponsor retains?
Can you show that you qualified each CRO and vendor before contract award and that you re-evaluate them on a defined cycle, with the qualification and re-evaluation records retained?

Do you have documented evidence of active sponsor oversight of CROs and vendors (governance meeting minutes, escalation records, KPI or metric review, and follow-up on issues) rather than only the contract and a kickoff meeting?
Is the sponsor's ultimate responsibility for the quality and integrity of the trial data, regardless of what is delegated, documented and reflected in your oversight records?

Do you have a documented monitoring plan for each trial that defines the monitoring approach (on-site, centralized, remote), frequency, and the specific data and processes to be verified, consistent with a risk-based strategy?
If you use centralized or risk-based monitoring, can you demonstrate the statistical or analytical checks performed, the signals they generated, and the actions taken in response?
Are monitoring visit reports completed within the timeframe defined in your procedures, reviewed by a responsible person, and is there documented follow-up to closure of the findings they raise?
Is the trial master file set up, indexed, and maintained contemporaneously throughout the trial (not assembled or reconciled only before an inspection or at database lock)?
