Do you have a TMF index or plan that defines the expected content, and do you run periodic completeness and quality reviews against it with the results documented?
For an electronic TMF, is the system validated, access-controlled by role, and does it retain a metadata audit trail showing who filed, moved, or replaced each document and when?
Is the split between the sponsor TMF and any site or investigator files clearly defined, and can you reconstruct the conduct of the trial from the combined records?

Are records retained for the full period required by the applicable regulation and protocol, with controls that prevent premature destruction and a documented retention or archival arrangement covering CRO-held records?
Has your EDC system been validated for its intended use, with documented user requirements, test evidence, and a release decision before it was used to capture trial data?
Does the EDC system enforce unique user accounts, role-based access, and removal of access when a person leaves the study, with periodic access reviews documented?

Does the EDC and any system holding GCP data maintain a secure, computer-generated, time-stamped audit trail that records the original value, the changed value, who made the change, when, and why, and is that audit trail reviewed on a risk basis?

When the EDC or related systems are configured or updated mid-study (edit checks, derivations, randomization), are those changes managed under change control and revalidated as appropriate before going live?
If electronic signatures are used on clinical records, do they meet the applicable Part 11 requirements and link uniquely to the signer so the signature cannot be repudiated or reused?

Do you have a documented source data verification or source data review approach that defines which data are verified, to what extent, and is the extent justified by your risk assessment rather than a blanket 100 percent by default?

Are source data identifiable and is it clear for each data point where the original record resides, including where eCRF entry itself is the source (direct data capture), with that designation documented in advance?
Can your trial data still be traced from source to the analysis dataset (the audit trail of the data flow is intact), so an investigator could follow a reported value back to its origin?
Do you have a documented process for capturing, classifying, reviewing, and reporting protocol deviations, and is each deviation assessed for impact on participant safety and data integrity?

Are deviations recorded contemporaneously and escalated and reported per protocol and regulation (for example, prompt reporting of deviations that affect safety or that were taken to protect a participant), rather than only summarized at study end?
Do you track protocol deviation trends across sites and use them to drive corrective and preventive actions?

For a blinded trial, is the blinding maintained by documented controls (separation of duties, restricted access to randomization and treatment assignment, blinded data review), and is unblinding limited to defined roles?
Do you have a controlled emergency unblinding procedure, and is every unblinding event (planned or emergency) logged with who, when, why, and reviewed for impact on the trial?
Is access to the randomization system and treatment codes restricted to authorized unblinded personnel, with access logs and audit trails available for review?

Do you have a safety reporting system that captures adverse events and serious adverse events, and can you demonstrate that SAE information moved from site to sponsor to safety database within the timelines your procedures require?

Are events that qualify for expedited safety reporting (US IND safety reports under 21 CFR 312.32 for serious and unexpected suspected adverse reactions, or EU SUSARs) assessed and submitted to regulators and investigators within the applicable regulatory timeframes (fatal or life-threatening within 7 calendar days and other qualifying events within 15 calendar days for US IND reports), with the clock-start documented?
Do you reconcile the clinical database against the safety (pharmacovigilance) database for SAEs on a defined schedule, with discrepancies resolved and documented before database lock?

Is there a documented data management plan, and is database lock preceded by defined activities (data review, query closure, SAE reconciliation, coding, audit-trail review) with a recorded lock decision and authorization?
Are medical coding (for example, MedDRA and WHODrug) and the coding dictionaries version-controlled, and is the coding reviewed by qualified personnel?

Do you confirm and document that participating investigators are qualified, that current financial disclosure information is collected and retained, and that the investigator agreed in writing to conduct the trial per protocol and GCP?

Do you have documented evidence that IRB or ethics committee approval (and approval of amendments) was obtained before the relevant activities started at each site, and that the approvals are filed in the TMF?

Do you confirm that valid informed consent using the current approved consent form was obtained and documented before any study procedures, and is this verified during monitoring with deviations handled as such?
Do you control investigational product accountability across the supply chain (shipment, storage conditions, dispensing, return, destruction) with reconciliation records that account for all units?

Do you have a process to detect and respond to serious or persistent noncompliance and potential fraud or data fabrication at a site, including for-cause audits and, where warranted, escalation to regulators?
Is your clinical staff trained on the protocol, GCP, and relevant SOPs with current training records, and is training repeated when the protocol or procedures are amended?

Do you have an independent clinical quality assurance audit program (sites, vendors, systems, the TMF) that is risk-based, with audit findings tracked to closure and reported to management, kept separate from routine monitoring?
